entry.title: The refreshed SAP Integration Certification Framework is now available
entry.content:
After months of close collaboration with our partner ecosystem, the SAP Integration and Certification Center (ICC) is proud to announce that the refreshed SAP Integration Certification Framework is officially live now.

This is a significant milestone — not just for SAP ICC, but for every partner building solutions on the SAP platform. The refreshed framework is the result of real conversations, real feedback, and a shared commitment to making integration certification more accessible, more relevant, and more aligned with where SAP and its partners are heading.

Why We Refreshed the Framework

The integration landscape has changed fundamentally. The rise of Business AI, the shift to Clean Core, the evolution of SAP Business AI Platform, and the introduction of new API policy all demanded a certification framework that could keep pace.

The previous framework served us well — but it was built for a different era. We set out to build something that reflects today's partner reality: diverse solution types, varied maturity levels, diverse system landscapes and an ecosystem that spans from established global partners to innovative niche specialists entering the market.

The result is a framework that is structured yet flexible, rigorous yet accessible.

Two Paths. One Goal.

At the heart of the refreshed framework is a dual-path model — two transparent paths designed to meet partners where they are.

SAP Partner Solution Certification Framework 2026 — Two transparent paths

Of the two pathways, Integration Certification is more closely aligned with SAP product architecture strategy. It is designed for applications built on SAP Business AI Platform that meet a defined set of technical standards: use of published APIs, adherence to Clean Core principles, connectivity to SAP Business Data Cloud, and — where applicable — built with SAP Business AI.

Integration Certification is based on endorsed use-cases and technical criteria by SAP. SAP ICC certifies against a defined portfolio of scenarios available at SAP Partner Portal, meaning the partner application shall map to one of those scenarios to be eligible.

The updated framework is aligned with SAP API policy. A certified solution is not just technically validated — it is a direct confirmation that the solution adheres to the design time guidelines of the new SAP API policy, giving customers and partners a clear and trusted signal of compliance.

Successfully certified solutions receive the SAP Certified logo and a certificate, are listed in the SAP Certified Solutions Directory, and gain access to go-to-market support through SAP Store — making certification not just a quality mark, but a tangible business advantage. Integration Certification continues to be a mandatory requirement for higher solution progression levels in SAP PartnerEdge (Spotlight+, Endorsed App).

Interoperability Review is a new, purposeful entry point into the integration certification framework — and one of the most requested suggestion from our partner and the broader community. Certain partner solutions are not able to qualify for Integration Certification and fit a predefined certification scenario. That's not a dead end – it's what the Interoperability Review is built for.

Interoperability Review is SAP ICC's open and inclusive validation path, available to partners and non-partners (ISVs) alike, regardless of where you sit in the SAP ecosystem. It is designed for applications that use published APIs correctly but integrate via methods or platforms that fall outside the SAP Integration Certification Framework – and it's open to standard, market-ready products.

SAP ICC assesses your application against interoperability standards: correct use of published APIs as documented, successful authentication and connectivity, correct data exchange, and appropriate error handling. The review confirms technical compatibility – without requiring deep platform alignment.

If the partner solution passes the Interoperability Review, the solution is listed as interoperable on SAP Notes, and receive a Review Summary documenting the outcome of the technical assessment. With these outcomes, partners can demonstrate to joint customers that the solution is compatible with SAP suite of solutions.

These two paths are not competing options. They are complementary instruments that together ensure every partner has a supported route to market for their solution through a technical validation by SAP.

What Happens Next

The framework is now live. Here is how to get started:

  1. Explore the two paths — review the full documentation ICC pages at SAP.com and SAP Partner Portal
  2. Understand the Certification Framework Reference Guide and FAQ
  3. Talk to us — contact the ICC team (icc@sap.com) to discuss which path is the right fit for your solution or apply for our service
  4. Start your journey — the process begins with a short discovery conversation with the ICC team.

A Thank You to Our Partner Community

This framework did not emerge from a room at SAP. It emerged from the conversations we have had with many partners — at partner events, in certification reviews, in feedback sessions, and from the direct input you have shared with us over the years.

To everyone who participated in shaping this: thank you. You will see your contribution reflected in what we have built with the refreshed framework.

We are proud of this framework. We are more proud of what it makes possible for you and your customers. We at SAP ICC are eager to see it go live and really making a change for our partner community.

The SAP ICC Team
SAP Integration and Certification Center

Have questions? Reach out to the ICC team at icc@sap.com

entry.title: Re: LOSC Putaway: Second WT Gets Cancelled After Confirmi...
entry.content:
Hello,

Thank you very much for your question. I'm very sorry for the delay.

#GenAI-Assisted Content

I asked our internal chatbot for support, and here's the response. Please check whether the suggestions help you resolve your issue:

Troubleshooting Inbound LOSC Process in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud EWM

It seems you are experiencing issues with the inbound LOSC process where the second Warehouse Task (WT) is automatically canceled after confirming the first WT, leaving the Handling Unit (HU) stuck in the intermediate storage bin. Here are some steps and considerations that might help resolve this issue:

Possible Causes and Resolutions

  1. Storage Type Configuration:

    • Ensure that the storage types are correctly configured for LOSC. The intermediate storage type should allow for the transition to the final putaway storage type.
    • Check if the ‘Confirm Putaway’ tab is flagged for the storage type Wrong Completion Status in the Change Inbound Deliveries EWM APP associated with the inbound delivery item. This ensures that the putaway can be completed only on a final bin.

  2. Warehouse Process Type Settings:

    • Verify that the warehouse process type settings are correctly defined. The ‘Storage Control Relevance’ should be set appropriately Warehouse process type for distributive putaway is not defined correctly to manage the transition between storage types.
    • Ensure that the flag ‘Confirm Immediately’ is set for the defined Warehouse Process Type Preallocated Stock in SAP S/4HANA Cloud WM - SAP Community (WPT).

  3. Configuration for Inbound Deliveries:

    • Check if the configuration for inbound deliveries is set to allow automatic creation and confirmation of WTs. This might involve reviewing the settings for ‘Automatic WT Creation at GR’. The putaway warehouse task is created automatically regardless storage type and warehouse process type settings

  4. Intermediate Storage Type Behavior:

    • Confirm that the intermediate storage type is not set to cancel subsequent tasks automatically. This might be a configuration issue where the intermediate storage type is not correctly linked to the final putaway storage type.

  5. Comparison with Outbound LOSC:

    • Since the outbound LOSC process works as expected, compare the configurations for inbound and outbound processes to identify any discrepancies.

Additional Suggestions

  • Testing and Validation: Conduct tests with different configurations to isolate the issue. This might involve changing the sequence of storage types or adjusting the warehouse process type settings.
  • Consult SAP Documentation: Review SAP documentation and community forums for similar issues and solutions.

If these steps do not resolve the issue, consider reaching out to SAP support for further assistance.

 

For more details, see the following information:

[1] Wrong Completion Status in the Change Inbound Deliveries EWM APP
[2] Warehouse process type for distributive putaway is not defined correctly
[3] Preallocated Stock in SAP S/4HANA Cloud WM - SAP Community
[4] The putaway warehouse task is created automatically regardless storage type and warehouse process type settings

Kind regards,

Henrike

entry.title: Build an Auto-Rotating Image Slider in SAPUI5 using Carousel Control.
entry.content:
Hello everyone,

Introduction  

While working on one of my SAPUI5 applications, I wanted the landing page to look more attractive instead of displaying a static image.  

After exploring different approaches, I found that combining the Carousel control with JavaScript's setInterval() function provides a simple and effective solution. In this blog, I'll share how I implemented an auto-rotating image slider and explain the logic behind it so that you can use the same approach in your SAPUI5 applications.  

The sap.m.Carousel control is a container control in SAPUI5 that allows users to navigate through a collection of content items. Each item within the Carousel can be an image, form, layout, or any other SAPUI5 control.  

Overview  

The Carousel control is commonly used for displaying image galleries, but it can also contain any sap.m control.  

Structure  

The carousel consists of the following elements:  

  • Content area - displays the different items.  
  • Navigation - arrows to the left and right for switching between items.  
  • Paging (optional) - indicator at the bottom to show the current position in the set.  

The paging indicator behaves as follows:  

  • showPageIndicator: Determines whether the indicator is displayed.  
  • If the dataset contains less than 9 pages, the indicator displays as bullets.  
  • If the dataset contains 9 or more pages, the indicator becomes numeric.  
  • pageIndicatorPlacement: Determines where the indicator is located. The default setting is sap.m.CarouselPageIndicatorPlacementType.Bottom (below the content).  

Step 1 :   

Create the SAPUI5 Project Using a Basic Template  

Step 2 :  

Configure the XML View (View1.view.xml)  

In this view:  

  • The left side contains the image slider using the Carousel control.   
  • The right side contains Login and Sign Up forms using IconTabBar.   

I chose this layout because it resembles a common login page design used in many business applications. Although the focus of this blog is the Carousel, placing it beside a login form demonstrates a practical use case. 

<mvc:View controllerName="carouselcontrolproject.controller.View1"
    xmlns:mvc="sap.ui.core.mvc"
    xmlns="sap.m" 
    xmlns:form="sap.ui.layout.form">
    <Page id="page" title="{i18n>title}">
        <HBox width="100%" justifyContent="SpaceBetween">
            <Carousel id="id1" loop="true" width="600px" height="500px">
                <Image src="https://images.pexels.com/photos/16767121/pexels-photo-16767121.jpeg" />
                <Image src="https://images.pexels.com/photos/32162192/pexels-photo-32162192.jpeg" />
                <Image src="https://images.pexels.com/photos/32228687/pexels-photo-32228687.jpeg" />
                <Image src="https://images.pexels.com/photos/32730210/pexels-photo-32730210.jpeg" />
            </Carousel>
            <VBox width="50%">
                <IconTabBar>
                    <items>
                        <IconTabFilter text="Login">
                            <form:SimpleForm editable="true" layout="ResponsiveGridLayout">
                                <form:content>
                                    <Label text="Email" />
                                    <Input id="inputLEmail" value="" valueState="None" valueStateText="" liveChange="onLiveChangeLEmail" />
                                    
                                    <Label text="Password" />
                                    <Input id="inputLPassword" value="" valueState="None" valueStateText="" type="Password" liveChange="onLiveChangeLPass" />
                                    
                                    <Label />
                                    <VBox alignItems="Center">
                                        <Button text="Log in" type="Emphasized" press="onPressLogin" />
                                    </VBox>
                                    
                                    <Label />
                                    <Link text="Forgot Password?" press="onPressOpenForPassFrag" />
                                </form:content>
                            </form:SimpleForm>
                        </IconTabFilter>
                        <IconTabFilter text="Sign up">
                            <form:SimpleForm editable="true" layout="ResponsiveGridLayout">
                                <form:content>
                                    <Label text="Name" />
                                    <Input id="inputSName" value="" valueStateText="" liveChange="onLiveChangeName" />
                                    
                                    <Label text="Email" />
                                    <Input id="inputSEmail" value="" valueStateText="" liveChange="onLiveChangeEmail" />
                                    
                                    <Label text="Password" />
                                    <Input id="inputSPassword" type="Password" value="" valueStateText="" liveChange="onLiveChangePass" />
                                    
                                    <Label text="Confirm Password" />
                                    <Input id="inputCPassword" type="Password" value="" valueStateText="" />
                                    
                                    <Label />
                                    <HBox justifyContent="SpaceBetween">
                                        <Button text="Reset" type="Emphasized" press="resetSFields" />
                                        <Button text="Sign Up" type="Emphasized" press="onPressSignUp" />
                                    </HBox>
                                </form:content>
                            </form:SimpleForm>
                        </IconTabFilter>
                    </items>
                </IconTabBar>
            </VBox>
        </HBox>
    </Page>
</mvc:View>

In my example, I configured the Carousel with loop="true" so that the images continue rotating without interruption. I also assigned a fixed width and height to maintain a consistent layout beside the login form. Each Image control represents one slide in the Carousel. 

Note: Since external image URLs are used from Pexels, an active internet connection is required to load the images. 

Step 3: 

Implement Auto-Rotation Logic (View1.controller.js) 

sap.ui.define([
    "sap/ui/core/mvc/Controller"
], function (Controller) {
    "use strict";

    return Controller.extend("carouselcontrolproject.controller.View1", {
        onInit: function () {
            // Fetch the Carousel control instance
            var oCarousel = this.byId("id1");
            
            // Set up a safe interval function for auto-rotation
            this._iIntervalId = setInterval(function () {
                if (oCarousel) {
                    oCarousel.next();
                }
            }, 1000);
        },

        onExit: function () {
            // Clean up the interval when the view is destroyed to prevent memory leaks
            if (this._iIntervalId) {
                clearInterval(this._iIntervalId);
            }
        }
    });
});

I used JavaScript's setInterval() function because it provides a simple way to execute the same action repeatedly. Every second, it calls the Carousel's next() method, allowing the images to rotate automatically without any user interaction. 

Note : For demonstration purposes, the interval is set to 1 second. In production applications, a value between 3 and 5 seconds generally provides a better user experience. 

Step-by-Step Execution 

  1. View loads  
  2. onInit() executes automatically  
  3. Carousel with ID id1 is fetched  
  4. The setInterval() callback function executes.  
  5. The next() method is called on the Carousel.  
  6. The Carousel navigates to the next image.  
  7. If the last image is reached and loop="true" is enabled, navigation continues from the first image. 

Output 

The application displays: 

  • An auto-rotating image slider on the left side  
  • Login and Sign Up forms on the right side  
  • Images automatically change every second using JavaScript interval logic 

Conclusion 

While implementing this feature, I realized that SAPUI5 already provides everything needed to build a simple image slider without relying on external UI libraries. By combining the standard sap.m.Carousel control with a small amount of JavaScript, I was able to create an auto-rotating banner that can easily be reused in landing pages, dashboards, or product showcase applications.

I hope this example helps anyone looking to add a more dynamic and engaging user interface to their SAPUI5 applications.

entry.title: Enable Talent Intelligence Hub without Data Migration
entry.content:
Hi Community,

We're planning to enable the Talent Intelligence Hub (TIH), but we'd like to avoid migrating our existing Center of Capabilities (CoC) data. Our goal is to use TIH as a clean starting point for a new competency framework.

To prepare for this, I used the DELIMIT functionality in Manage Job Profile Content Import/Export to remove competencies, libraries, categories, and related data before running the TIH upgrade. However, we noticed that the competencies were still migrated during the upgrade.

Has anyone experienced this before? Is it possible to activate TIH without migrating the existing CoC data? Or do you have any recommendations or best practices for starting with an empty Talent Intelligence Hub while designing a new competency framework?

Thanks in advance!

entry.title: Re: SAP DM - Unexpected storage location & quantity in ma...
entry.content:
#GenAI-Assisted Content

Hello,

Thank you very much for your question. I'm very sorry for the delay.

I asked our internal chatbot for support, and here's the response. Please check whether the suggestions help you resolve your issue:

Issue with Storage Location Determination in SAP Digital Manufacturing

In SAP Digital Manufacturing (Release 2605), there seems to be a discrepancy in how storage locations are determined for component consumption compared to the expected behavior in S/4HANA Public Cloud.

Expected Behavior

  • Storage Location Determination: In S/4HANA, the withdrawal storage location for a component can be defined in the bill of materials The production storage location in the material master is not copied into the production order (BOM), which overrides the storage location specified in the component’s material master.
  • Reservation Influence: This setting should influence the reservation for the material, ensuring that the correct storage location is used during component consumption.

Actual Behavior

  • API Usage: The API findMaterialsByPlantUsingGET_2 rigidly reads the storage location from the component’s material master, ignoring the BOM-specified issue storage location. Issuing storage location field is not filled in PR automatically by MRP
  • Quantity Retrieval: The API Stocks retrieves the stock quantity from a different storage location than expected (1110 instead of 1170).

Resolution

To align the behavior of the ‘Dialog Component Consumption’ plug-in with the storage location determination in S/4HANA Public Cloud, consider the following:

  • API Customization: Investigate if the API can be customized or extended to consider the BOM-specified storage location.
  • Configuration Review: Ensure that the configuration settings in SAP DM are correctly set to reflect the BOM settings.

Additional Resources

For more detailed guidance, refer to the documentation on Component Consumption Form and Storage Location Determination in BOM Explosion.

For more details, see the following information:

[1] The production storage location in the material master is not copied into the production order
[2] Issuing storage location field is not filled in PR automatically by MRP

Kind regards,

Henrike

entry.title: Re: Internal Manage Early Numbering
entry.content:
#GenAI-Assisted Content

Hello,

Thank you very much for your question. I'm very sorry for the delay.

I asked our internal chatbot for support, and here's the response. Please check whether the suggestions help you resolve your issue:

Troubleshooting Early Numbering Implementation

To address the issue with your early numbering implementation, follow these steps:

1. Verify Number Range Configuration

Ensure that the number range object ‘ZCLA’ and range ‘01’ are correctly configured 3106145 - Error Invalid Namespace for Sequential Number 2907910 - Listing configuration error while assigning step number in the system. You can check this in the SAP GUI under transaction SNRO.

2. Check Error Handling

Your method is correctly catching exceptions for cx_nr_object_not_found and cx_number_ranges. However, ensure that these exceptions are not being triggered due to misconfiguration or missing number range objects.

3. Debugging

To debug the issue further:

  • Use breakpoints in your method to inspect the values of number_range_key, number_range_return_code, and number_range_returned_quantity.
  • Check if the number_get method is returning the expected values.

4. Validate Mapped Table

Ensure that the mapped-zi_cla_exp_header table is correctly defined and accessible. You can add debugging statements or use breakpoints to verify that values are being appended correctly.

5. Manual Numbering

If manual numbering works, it indicates that the issue might be with the number range configuration or the way the number is being fetched. Double-check the configuration and ensure that the fiscal year and other parameters are correctly passed.

6. Review Behavior Definition

Ensure that the behavior definition aligns with your requirements and that the NumClaim field is correctly set as readonly and managed by the early numbering logic.

Example Debugging Code

You can add debugging statements like:

WRITE: / 'Number Range Key:', number_range_key.
WRITE: / 'Return Code:', number_range_return_code.
WRITE: / 'Returned Quantity:', number_range_returned_quantity.

This will help you see the output during execution.

Conclusion

By following these steps, you should be able to identify and resolve the issue with your early numbering implementation. If the problem persists, consider consulting SAP documentation or support for further assistance.

For more details, see the following information:

[1] 3106145 - Error Invalid Namespace for Sequential Number
[2] 2907910 - Listing configuration error while assigning step number

Kind regards,

Henrike

entry.title: Re: SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud in a construction or contrac...
entry.content:
#GenAI-Assisted Content

Hello,

Thank you very much for your question. I'm very sorry that nobody provided an answer so far. 

I asked our internal chatbot for support, and here's the response. Please check whether the suggestions help you resolve your issue:

SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud Implementation for Construction/EPC Contractors

Implementing SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud in construction or contracting companies, especially in regions like Saudi Arabia, UAE, or Egypt, involves several key areas such as project management, subcontracting, procurement, and cost control. Here are some insights based on available resources:

Project Management

SAP S/4HANA Cloud offers robust project management capabilities that can be tailored for construction projects. The system supports project-based engineer-to-order processes, Project based Engineer-to-Order with Two-Tier Integration in SAP S/4HANA which are crucial for managing complex construction projects. This includes integration between headquarters and subsidiaries, allowing for real-time updates and efficient resource utilization.

Subcontracting

For subcontracting, SAP S/4HANA Cloud provides solutions to manage subcontracting purchase orders with alternative component BOM explosions. Subcontracting PO with Alternative component BOM explosion in SAP S/4HANA This feature is particularly useful for construction companies that need to manage different versions of BOMs for subcontracting purposes.

Procurement

The procurement process in SAP S/4HANA Cloud is streamlined to handle complex requirements typical in construction projects. The system supports efficient management of procurement activities, ensuring timely delivery of materials and services.

Cost Control

Cost control is a critical aspect of construction projects, and SAP S/4HANA Cloud offers tools for real-time cost analysis and operational efficiency. This helps in aligning operational costs with company goals and improving revenue impact.

For more detailed insights and real-world examples, connecting with SAP community members who have implemented SAP S/4HANA Cloud in similar settings can be beneficial. You might also consider exploring SAP’s best practices and community resources for further guidance.


For more details, see the following information:

[1] Project based Engineer-to-Order with Two-Tier Integration in SAP S/4HANA
[2] Subcontracting PO with Alternative component BOM explosion in SAP S/4HANA

Kind regards,

Henrike

entry.title: Business Data Cloud & Datasphere: Are the Needs of Mid-Sized Companies Being Adequately Considered?
entry.content:
For the past one and a half years, we have been working on a migration project from a traditional SAP BW 7.5 system to SAP Datasphere. During this journey, we have experienced many positive developments. At the same time, however, we continue to encounter the same challenge: the technology often evolves faster than the associated licensing models that are economically viable for mid-sized companies.

About one and a half years ago we wanted to set up a Datasphere instance and start the migration. Since SAP Business Data Cloud had just been announced, it seemed natural not to sign a traditional Datasphere contract but instead to go directly with a BDC contract.

At that time, Business Data Cloud was still in its early stages, and many licensing details had not yet been fully clarified. As a customer, it sometimes felt as though we were among the first adopters, working alongside SAP to determine how certain licensing models could be implemented in practice from both a commercial and architectural perspective.

Today, we find ourselves facing a comparable challenge once again.

The Object Store as a Key Component

In our productive Datasphere environment, we are already using the Object Store. For several scenarios, particularly BW legacy data, data historization and future data product use cases, it represents an important building block for us. However, we currently store only around 60 GB of data there.

Looking ahead to future AI capabilities and a modern data architecture, the Object Store also appears to be a good choice. Anyone planning to build data products, AI applications, and a reliable data foundation will eventually have to address this topic.

We would now like to establish a second Datasphere instance for testing and development purposes.

This is where the challenge begins.

Based on our current understanding, the use of the Object Store is tied to a minimum configuration of 4,150 Capacity Units. While this may be reasonable for our productive environment, it is simply oversized for a small test system. For our requirements, the typical minimum configuration of approximately 1,500 Capacity Units for Datasphere would be more than sufficient.

In the test environment, we neither require years of historical data nor large storage capacities. Nevertheless, there currently seems to be no option to license the Object Store in a smaller configuration.

From an outside perspective, this appears more like a commercial limitation than a technical one. After all, SAP Data Products can also run on an Object Store. However, I am not an SAP developer; I am a Product Owner in a mid-sized company. Perhaps there are technical considerations that I am overlooking.

What Options Does a Mid-Sized Customer Have?

From our perspective, there are currently only three possible options:

1. Purchase a Significantly Larger Datasphere Test Instance Than Actually Required

This would allow us to use the Object Store, but the additional cost would be difficult to justify from a business perspective.

→ Economically not viable

2. Avoid Using the Object Store Altogether

This would prevent us from implementing certain data product and AI scenarios. In that case, we might have to wait one or two years until SAP provides a more suitable solution.

→ Not an ideal foundation for future innovation

3. Use the Object Store Only in the Productive Environment

This would introduce new challenges regarding development, testing, and transports between systems.

→ Maintainability and operational complexity would suffer significantly

None of these options appears particularly satisfactory.

I have already found an SAP Influence Request that addresses exactly this topic. However, for our current situation, it does not provide a practical solution, because it will be to late...

https://influence.sap.com/sap/ino/#idea/378537

For mid-sized companies in particular, this creates a substantial entry barrier. Many customers want to test innovative capabilities such as Data Products, AI applications, and the Object Store, but they do not require an enterprise-scale test environment.

Is a Mid-Market Option Missing?

From a customer perspective, the question arises whether SAP is adequately considering the needs of small and mid-sized businesses in this area.

Fundamentally, we are convinced by the strategic direction of Business Data Cloud and Datasphere. However, there is an impression that certain licensing models are primarily designed for larger customers and more extensive deployment scenarios.

A more flexible option for smaller environments could remove many barriers and significantly increase the adoption of new capabilities. Especially in innovation and proof-of-concept projects, organizations need the ability to experiment with new technologies in an economically reasonable and low-risk way before scaling them further.

Open Question to the Community

Perhaps we are overlooking an option, or perhaps other customers have already found ways to address similar challenges.

  • How are you dealing with this topic?
  • Have you found alternative approaches for test and development environments?
  • Are there ways to use the Object Store more economically?
  • Or have you encountered the same limitations?

I look forward to hearing about your experiences and engaging in a constructive discussion.

Our intention is not to criticize SAP in general. On the contrary, we want to successfully adopt Business Data Cloud and Datasphere and see significant potential in these new technologies. At the moment, however, we feel that a licensing model better aligned with the realities of mid-sized companies is still missing.

Best regards,
Niels

entry.title: Re: SAPUI5 Versions Ending Cloud Provisioning in Q3/26
entry.content:
這要看情況——如果你的應用程式運行在 SAP Work Zone 上，你可能不會遇到問題，因為據我所知，那裡始終使用的是最新的 SAPUI5 版本。

但是，如果您的應用程式在 BTP 上獨立運行，則應在 index.html 中檢查此應用程式啟動了哪個 SAPUI5。

另一個關鍵情況是，如果您從工作區的本機系統呼叫 SAPUI5 應用程式。您需要注意的是，您本機系統上使用的 SAPUI5 修補程式計劃會從 SAPUI5 CDN 中移除。否則，您需要將本機系統升級到更新的版本

(此訊息由機器翻譯)

entry.title: Route Determination for One-Time Customers in S/4HANA Public Cloud (EDI sales Orders)
entry.content:
Hello SAP Community Members,

We have a business requirement to determine the route automatically during sales order creation via the EDI channel for one-time customers in SAP S/4HANA Public Cloud.

In the standard route determination process, the route is derived based on factors such as the shipping point/departure transportation zone and the customer's transportation zone (ship-to party). However, for one-time customers, no permanent Ship-To Business Partner master data exists, which makes the standard approach difficult to use.

The main challenge is that the destination address varies with every order, so maintaining a fixed transportation zone in the Business Partner master data is not practical. Additionally, since this is an S/4HANA Public Cloud environment, we understand that SAP has restrictions around route determination updates through SOAP APIs.

We have already reviewed the BAdI SD_SLS_MODIFY_ITEM (Sales Document Item Modification) as a potential extensibility option. However, transportation zone and route fields do not appear to be available for modification within this BAdI.

Has anyone encountered a similar requirement? If so, could you please share how you addressed it? Are there any available extensibility options, workarounds, or alternative approaches to determine the route dynamically for one-time customers in S/4HANA Public Cloud?

Any suggestions or experiences would be greatly appreciated.

Thank you in advance!

entry.title: Re: PO Text Auto populate at Header text
entry.content:
#GenAI-Assisted Content

Hello,

Thank you very much for your question. I'm very sorry for the delay.

I asked our internal chatbot for support, and here's the response. Please check whether the suggestions help you resolve your issue:

Auto-Populating and Editing Header Text in Purchase Order

To have the Header Text auto-populated and editable for each text ID in the Purchase Order form using the ME21N screen, follow these steps:

Steps to Configure:

  1. Access SSCUI 102270: How to enable texts to be displayed on the purchase order outputs

    • Navigate to ‘Define Texts for Purchase Order’ in SSCUI 102270.
    • Here, you can define new text types for the Purchase Order header, such as Header Text, Warranty, and Shipping Details.

  2. Create New Entries:

    • Click on ‘New Entries’ to add new text types.
    • Ensure each text type has a unique print sequence number 3219347 - Missing header text in Purchase Order Output to avoid conflicts in the output.

  3. Maintain Standard Texts:

    • Use the ‘Maintain Standard Texts’ app to create standard texts that can be used in the Purchase Order.
    • Note down the Text Object, Text ID, and Text Name details.

  4. Configure Text Determination:

    • In the text determination procedure, set up text determination for the Purchase Order header.
    • Assign the text object, text ID, and text name to the respective access sequence.

  5. Editable Texts in ME21N:

    • Once configured, these texts will be auto-populated in the ME21N screen and can be edited as needed.

Notes:

  • Ensure that the text IDs are in the format Fxx or ST to be considered for PO Output.
  • If two text IDs have the same print sequence, only the first one will be printed.

By following these steps, you can ensure that the Header Text and other text types are auto-populated and editable in the Purchase Order form.

 
For more details, see the following information:
[1] How to enable texts to be displayed on the purchase order outputs
[2] 3219347 - Missing header text in Purchase Order Output

Kind regards,

Henrike

entry.title: Re: Incorrect VAT reporting date transferred from SD to FI in Public Cloud
entry.content:
Hi Chris,

Thank you for the detailed answer.

Please find some additional information on the topics below.

Scenario 1: Cancellation invoice We are well aware of the mentioned BAdI, Reversal: Tax Reporting Date Derivation. However, it is not called in the Hungarian localization, so we created a ticket for this.

According to the information we received, the Hungarian localization contains special programmed logic that cannot be changed. SAP also stated that this programmed logic is in accordance with Hungarian law.

Scenario 2: Collective invoice The described date determination is defined by Hungarian law. For tax reporting, the maximum date should be used. However, this does not work in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition. In the derivation rules, we could not find any setting for this. In addition, the BAdIs described in the documentation for the derivation rules are not available in Public Cloud. For NAV Online invoice reporting to the Hungarian tax authority, there is a BAdI where this behavior can be implemented. However, for tax declaration purposes, we could not find a solution.

Scenario 3: Final invoice with down payment Yes, we also believe that this must be a programming error. We have created a ticket for this issue as well.

Best regards, Gabor

entry.title: SAP Document AI – Integration from Multiple External Web Systems (PDF Upload) & Response Data / Rete
entry.content:
Hi all,

We are currently designing an integration architecture involving SAP Document AI and would appreciate some clarification from the community or SAP experts.

We operate multiple external web systems, and users upload PDF documents through each of these systems. We are evaluating whether these PDFs can be sent to SAP Document AI for processing (OCR / data extraction), as part of a broader automation/AI transformation initiative we're currently designing.

### Questions

**1. Feasibility**

Is it possible for PDF documents uploaded from multiple external web systems to be sent to SAP Document AI via API for processing (OCR/data extraction)?

**2. Response data and reusability**

When calling the API, what data is returned in the response (e.g., extracted fields, confidence scores, whether the original document is included, etc.)?

- Is this response meant to be consumed immediately by the calling system only (synchronous, one-time use)?
- Or is the extraction result persisted on the SAP Document AI side, so that it can be retrieved later by a different system or at a different point in time (i.e., queried again after the fact)?

**3. Document retention**

Is the original PDF file itself stored on the SAP side after being submitted? If so, how long is it retained (e.g., indefinitely, or deleted after a certain period)?

---

Any insight — whether from documentation, hands-on experience, or official SAP guidance — would be greatly appreciated.

Thanks in advance!

entry.title: Re: How to Link Two Analytical Queries in Embedded SAC Ap...
entry.content:
#GenAI-Assisted Content

Hello,

Thank you very much for your question. 

I asked our internal chatbot for support, and here's the response. Please check whether the suggestions help you resolve your issue:

How to Link Two Analytical Queries in Embedded SAC App

When you encounter the error ‘A linked dimension for this model is not included in the visualization’s dimension bindings,’ it typically indicates that the dimension you are trying to link is not properly configured in the visualization settings. Here are steps to resolve this issue:

  1. Verify Dimension Binding:

    • Ensure that the dimension you are linking (e.g., Company Code) is included in the visualization’s dimension bindings. This means checking if the dimension is correctly mapped in the widget settings.

  2. Check Dimension Properties:

    • Confirm that both Company Code fields in the queries have the same data type, semantics, and 3038787 - Fail to create Analytical Dimension with error message ‘Element xxx must have an association with master data view’ - Custom CDS Views values. This is crucial for successful linking.

  3. Adjust Visualization Settings:

    • Go to the visualization settings in the SAC application and ensure that the linked dimension is Customer use case of Embedded SAC on Sales Gross P… - SAP Community selected and properly bound to the visualization.

  4. Review Linked Dimensions Configuration:

    • Sometimes additional configuration is required for linked dimensions. Ensure that the linking is correctly set up in the SAC model settings.

  5. Consult Documentation:

    • Refer to SAP documentation or community forums for specific guidance on linking dimensions in SAC applications.

If these steps do not resolve the issue, it may be helpful to consult SAP support or community forums for more detailed troubleshooting specific to your setup.


For more details, see the following information:

[1] 3038787 - Fail to create Analytical Dimension with error message 'Element xxx must have an association with master data view' - Custom CDS Views
[2] Customer use case of Embedded SAC on Sales Gross P... - SAP Community
Using Data Bindings
Enabling Linked Analysis for Your Custom Widget (Optimized Story Experience)

Kind regards,

Henrike

entry.title: Restricting Cross-Day Pairing for Missing Punch in SuccessFactors Time Tracking CICO
entry.content:
Introduction

Customers enable the setting "Enable pairing of Time Events of the same Time Event Type" under the Clock In Clock Out (CICO) settings page.

However, this setting introduces a major operational challenge: if an employee forgets to clock out at the end of Day 1, the system's pairing engine will automatically look ahead and pair that last "Clock In" with the first "Clock In" of Day 2. This results in massive, unrealistic cross-day working blocks (often 20+ hours long) appearing on the employee’s timesheet.

Time sheet - missing punch (20+ working hours)

 
This blog details a technical workaround developed to stop this cross-day pairing at the data layer.

The Breakthrough Workaround: Validating the External Time Data Object

To catch and block these incorrect pairings you can validate the data directly at the MDF object.

Object - External Time Data

 

Step 1: Enable End Date Field

  1. Navigate to Configure Object Definitions.
  2. Select the MDF Object: ExternalTimeData.
  3. Locate the field endDate. By default its visibility is set to Not Visible.
  4. Switch the field visibility to Editable or Visible so it can be evaluated by the rules engine.


Step 2: Create the Cross-Day Validation Business Rule

Create an MDF business rule configured under the External Time Data base object to evaluate whether a calculated pair crosses calendar day boundary.
Configure your rule (Validate_Cross_Days_Records) as follows:

  • Base Object: External Time Data
  • Purpose: Evaluate
  • Rule Logic:

Business Rule

 

Step 3: Assign the Rule to the Object

Return to Configure Object Definitions for ExternalTimeData and assign this rule as a Save Rule.

System Behavior After Implementation

Once this rule is active, the system halts the cross-day calculation before it updates the timesheet.

The Timesheet View

Time Sheet - No Pairing with the day after

 

 

Instead of generating a massive 23-hour working block, the shift stays cleanly separated. The un-paired punch remains isolated on its respective day, throwing a standard warning message ("The time event has not been paired for X hours"), which clearly tells the user or supervisor that a clock out was missed.


The Admin Alerts View

Because the save rule triggers an Error severity, any invalid cross-day pairing attempt is caught and routed to the Admin Alerts console under "Processing Paired Time Events for Time Sheet".

Admin Alert

 

 

The alert log displays your exact custom error message ("Reason for failure: Cross Day is not allowed"), making it highly trackable for administrators to identify and address missed punches.

Conclusion

Finding the sweet spot between employee flexibility and robust timesheet governance is entirely achievable. While the standard CICO engine will automatically pair events up to 24 hours apart, this default behavior leaves your system exposed to bad data whenever an employee forgets a punch.
By implementing the boundaries and validation checks outlined above, you ensure that employees can transition between tasks seamlessly, while your system proactively safeguards against accidental cross-day pairings. The result is an automated, self-healing time system that delivers 100% reliable data to payroll.

entry.title: Re: 创建销项发票界面coding block扩展字段配置
entry.content:
Hi @JuliaLu 

Short answer: Coding block can be created, but only in a very narrow way — the only supported route is the business context Coding Block (FINS_CODING_BLOCK), the field shows only on G/L account line items, and there is a cap on the number of fields you can create.

Which business context to select

Use Accounting: Coding Block (technical name FINS_CODING_BLOCK) in the Custom Fields app. Coding Block is the context whose values can be entered manually on the UI and are then passed into the journal entry and to follow-on documents. Reference: Custom Fields for Journal Entries Used in G/L Accounting

Do not use Accounting: Journal Entry Item for this requirement — values in that context are derived by the system and cannot be typed in by the user. Accounting: Market Segment is only for margin analysis characteristics. Reference: KBA 3041720

What to enable on the UI side

Create Outgoing Invoices is not a Fiori/OData app — it is the classic transaction FB70 rendered as SAP GUI for HTML in the launchpad (SAP Fiori Apps Reference Library – Create Outgoing Invoices). Therefore, it does not appear as a selectable UI/OData service in the User Interfaces tab of the Custom Fields app. After you create the field and publish it, the Coding Block field is picked up automatically in the account assignment part of the G/L line item — there is no per-app "Enable Usage" switch for FB70.

Practical tip: maintain the medium label and short label in the UI Texts section — those are the texts used by SAP GUI applications, whereas Fiori uses label/tooltip. Reference: Editing Custom Fields

What to enable for CDS views / reporting

CDS enablement is not needed to make the field visible in FB70. It is needed only if you want the value in reporting or APIs: open the published field and use the Analytics tab (data sources / CDS views, e.g. journal entry line item views) and the APIs, Form Templates or Email Templates tabs as required, then Publish again. Reference: Editing Custom Fields

Coding Block context overall: maximum 20 custom fields, 400 characters in total, data types limited to CHAR and NUMC, and the fields are relevant for G/L account line items only — not for the customer (AR) line, AP items or asset accounting. Reference: Extensibility – how to implement Custom Fields using Coding Block business context

If this limit does not fit your requirement

Consider posting via Post General Journal Entries / Upload General Journal Entries / Manage Journal Entries, where Coding Block fields are fully supported and can be enabled explicitly under User Interfaces > Services > Enable Usage (same blog above). If your "outgoing invoice" is actually an SD billing document (Create Billing Documents, F0798), the correct contexts are Sales: Billing Document / Sales: Billing Document Item, and there the UI enablement for the Fiori apps does exist: Process Extensibility for Sales Documents and Billing Documents

Summary

Create the field in the Custom Fields app with business context Accounting: Coding Block, maintain the short/medium labels, and Publish — it then appears on the G/L line items of Create Outgoing Invoices with no app-specific UI activation. Use the Analytics tab only if you also need the field in CDS-based reporting. If you need a field on the customer line, Create Outgoing Invoice is the wrong app for that requirement.

Hope it helps.
Best regards,
Michal

entry.title: Blog#9: How text templates are used and configured in SAP Cloud ALM
entry.content:
In my last post, I shared how custom mail notifications keep the right people informed in SAP Cloud ALM. This time it is about the content of the tickets themselves. With pony, text templates give every new Requirement a defined starting structure, so the description quality does not depend on who writes it.

Every Requirement needs a description that the next person can work with. What belongs in it depends on the process, and every author has their own style of writing it down. A text template changes the starting point. Instead of a blank field, the author begins with the structure the process expects, already filled into the description.


Let's be specific: A description template based on the scope of a Requirement

Here is a concrete example from one of my projects. We create a new Requirement and enter a title. Under "Additional Information", the "Scope" field holds the business process the Requirement belongs to, in our example "Order to Cash".

The moment the scope is selected, the description is filled automatically with the template that belongs to it. The business analyst completes the pre-filled text instead of starting from scratch, and every "Order to Cash" Requirement now begins with the same structure.

Description filled automatically based on the scope selection

 

More than one template: a structure for each scope

The example above uses one scope, but the same mechanism covers the whole process landscape:

  • A separate template can be stored for each scope, for example "P2P", "O2C", "R2R" and "P2M"
  • The template reacts to the field value, so the right structure appears the moment the scope is chosen
  • The template text is part of the configuration and can be adjusted at any time, without touching the process
  • Behind it runs the same workflow framework I shared in my post about consistency checks, so the conditions can be shaped the same way

This way, the writing guideline that used to live in a concept document becomes part of the ticket itself, right at the moment the content is created.

How it is configured, without code

Text templates are set up in a graphical editor. No developer required.

In the pony administration, we find the "Workflows" app. For our example, there is a workflow called "Set Template Text in Requirements", built in the same three logical steps as the previous examples:

  • Step 1 - When the workflow runs: the trigger, in our example a change in a Requirement
  • Step 2 - What is verified: the check, in our example which scope is entered, with one branch per scope ("P2P", "O2C", "R2R", "P2M")
  • Step 3 - What happens: the action behind each branch enters the template text that belongs to the scope

Once the workflow is published, it is active immediately. From that moment, every new "Order to Cash" Requirement starts with the "Order to Cash" structure.

Details of the Workflow

 

In summary: text templates make sure a Requirement starts with the structure your process expects. The description is complete and comparable across the team, the business analyst fills in content instead of inventing a format, and the guideline is applied exactly where the work happens. The standard SAP Cloud ALM process keeps running as before, the content just starts well-structured.

In my next post, I will share how enhanced authorization controls bring more fine-grained permissions to SAP Cloud ALM.

entry.title: Re: Banking Payments paid Invoices posting dates of Invoi...
entry.content:
Hi, @kedalenechong 

I tested it again. The join with T1.DocLine returns only the first invoice row because RCT2.DocLine does not always correspond to INV1.LineNum.

To return the G/L account for every invoice row, use:

LEFT JOIN INV1 T5
ON T5.DocEntry = T2.DocEntry

Then add:

T5.AcctCode AS 'GL Account'

This returns one row for each invoice line, including invoices containing multiple rows with different G/L accounts.

Best regards.

entry.title: Re: Incorrect VAT reporting date transferred from SD to FI in Public Cloud
entry.content:
Hi Chris,

Thank you for the detailed answer.

Please find some additional information regarding the three scenarios below.

Scenario 1: Cancellation invoice

We are well aware of the mentioned BAdI, Reversal: Tax Reporting Date Derivation. However, it is not called in the Hungarian localization. We have already opened SAP ticket 943389/2026 regarding this issue.

SAP informed us that the Hungarian localization contains special programmed logic that cannot be changed. According to SAP, this programmed logic is compliant with Hungarian legislation.

Scenario 2: Collective invoice

The date determination described above is required under Hungarian legislation. For tax reporting purposes, the latest fulfilment date should be used. However, this does not work correctly in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.

We could not find any corresponding settings in the derivation rules, and the BAdIs described in the derivation rule documentation are not available in the Public Cloud.

For NAV Online Invoice reporting to the Hungarian tax authority, a BAdI is available through which this behaviour can be programmed. However, we could not find a similar solution for VAT return reporting.

Scenario 3: Final invoice with down payment

Yes, we also believe that this must be a programming error. We have created SAP ticket 938937/2026 regarding this issue.

Best regards,
Gabor

entry.title: Building CPI Explorer – My Journey to Simplify Searching SAP Integration Suite iFlows
entry.content:
Building CPI Explorer – My Journey to Simplify Searching SAP Integration Suite iFlows

Introduction

As an SAP Integration Suite consultant, a large part of my day involves supporting existing integrations, troubleshooting production issues, and understanding how different systems are connected. One challenge kept coming up repeatedly: finding where a particular SFTP directory, HTTP endpoint, ProcessDirect endpoint, or credential was configured across dozens (sometimes hundreds) of Integration Flows.

SAP's Design-Time APIs let you retrieve Integration Packages and Integration Flows, but there's no built-in way to search adapter configurations across an entire tenant. In practice, that meant opening package after package and manually inspecting each iFlow — slow, repetitive, and easy to get wrong in a large landscape.

Instead of continuing to do this by hand, I decided to build a tool to solve it for myself, and hopefully for other developers too.

Where It All Started

I started my career as an ABAP developer, and one feature I relied on constantly was the "Where Used" list. If I needed to know where a table, function module, or field was being used, I could just look it up. It was simple, fast, and always available.

When I moved into PI/PO and later CPI, that convenience disappeared. There's no equivalent way to instantly search where something is used across your integration landscape.

This became a real problem in day-to-day support. An FICO or SD colleague would come to me and say "my file isn't moving, can you check?" Half the time, they weren't even sure whether their interface was flowing through PI/CPI at all — they just knew something was stuck and assumed I'd know where to look. Other times, someone from the infra team would ask "are we using this host anywhere? We need to update it" or "can you find every flow using this username, we're rotating credentials." Each of these meant manually opening iFlow after iFlow, hoping I didn't miss one.

That's where the idea for CPI Explorer actually came from — not as a big architectural vision, but as a way to get back something as basic as the ABAP "Where Used" list, just for CPI.

One real example was during an infrastructure change where we had to update an SFTP host. Before CPI Explorer, the only option was to manually open multiple Integration Flows and inspect every SFTP adapter. With CPI Explorer, I can simply search for the hostname and immediately see every Integration Flow that references it.

The Idea

The question I started with was simple:

 

                   Can I build a "Where Used" list for CPI?

I spent quite some time exploring the SAP Business Accelerator Hub and Design-Time APIs to see whether this functionality already existed. The Design-Time APIs gave me metadata, but not the adapter-level detail I needed. So I went a level deeper: download each Integration Flow artifact, extract the .iflw file, parse the XML, and build a local searchable index from it.

Once that was working, I wrapped it in a Chrome Extension so I could search directly from the browser instead of running scripts every time.

How CPI Explorer Works

At a high level, the tool:

  • Authenticates with SAP Integration Suite using OAuth
  • Retrieves Integration Packages and Integration Flows via the Design-Time APIs
  • Downloads Integration Flow artifacts
  • Extracts and parses the .iflw XML files
  • Builds a searchable metadata index
  • Lets you search adapter properties from the Chrome Extension

It currently supports searching for:

  • SFTP directories
  • Host names
  • HTTP endpoints
  • ProcessDirect endpoints
  • JMS queues
  • Credentials
  • Externalized parameters
  • Other adapter properties

What I Learned

Building this taught me more than I expected about:

  • SAP Integration Suite Design-Time APIs
  • The structure of Integration Flow artifacts
  • Parsing .iflw XML files
  • Chrome Extension development
  • Organizing a Node.js application into reusable modules

The trickiest part was making sure every downloaded artifact mapped back correctly to its source Integration Flow while the index was being built. Getting that mapping right was what made the search results trustworthy across a whole tenant.

Why I'm Sharing This

I built CPI Explorer to solve my own problem, but I believe many SAP Integration Suite developers face the same challenge. That's why I decided to make it open source, so others can use it, contribute ideas, and help improve it over time.

GitHub

The project is available here: https://github.com/lokeshbhukya2019-netizen/cpi-explorer

Feedback, suggestions, and contributions are welcome.

What's Next

A few things I'm planning to add:

  • Search by adapter type
  • Multi-tenant support
  • Export to Excel/CSV
  • Dependency visualization
  • Support for additional adapter types

If you have ideas for other useful features, I'd like to hear them.

Closing Thoughts

CPI Explorer started as an attempt to fix a problem I ran into constantly in day-to-day SAP Integration Suite work. Along the way it turned into a good excuse to learn more about SAP's APIs, artifact structures, and browser extension development.

I hope it saves other SAP Integration Suite developers some time during troubleshooting and impact analysis. If you try it, I'd genuinely like to hear how it goes.

Thank you for taking the time to read about CPI Explorer. If you try it out, I'd love to hear your feedback, suggestions, or ideas for new features. I hope it helps make troubleshooting and impact analysis in SAP Integration Suite a little easier for the community.

Happy integrating!

entry.title: Redesigning Work for Human–AI Collaboration – An OCM Blueprint
entry.content:
“AI is live” is not the same as “AI is delivering value.” The difference usually lives in the workflow. If roles, steps, and decisions haven’t changed, you’re probably running pre‑AI processes on top of AI tools. Value doesn’t come from turning features on. It comes from redesigning how humans and AI collaborate.

 

When “AI Is Live” Still Feels Like “Before”

Many organisations have now had their first AI programme experience. The pattern is familiar:

  • The model goes live.

  • Training sessions happen.

  • Dashboards show promising adoption numbers.

Yet when you look at cost, quality, cycle time, or risk, the improvement is modest at best.

Dig into the day‑to‑day work and a consistent theme emerges: the workflow never really changed. AI is present in the process, but the process itself is still designed for a pre‑AI world.

OCM’s job is to help teams do the harder work: redesigning how work flows so AI capability and human capability actually combine.

 

Why Workflow Redesign Matters Now

Previous waves of automation mainly targeted individual steps. A manual action was replaced; a recurring task was removed.

AI changes something deeper. It introduces prediction, summarisation, classification, and recommendation at multiple points. That shifts the logic of the whole process:

  • Bottlenecks move and new ones emerge.

  • Human‑only tasks become hybrid.

  • Role boundaries change even when job titles stay the same.

  • The “right” sequence of steps is different when you can anticipate issues or generate outputs early.

If you simply plug AI into the legacy flow without redesigning, people will work around the system and the value will stay theoretical.

 

A Four‑Step Blueprint for Human–AI Workflow Design

You don’t need a complex methodology to start doing this well. You need a simple blueprint and the discipline to apply it consistently.

1. Map the AI Touchpoints

First, get concrete. Instead of talking about “AI in finance” or “AI in customer service”, identify exact touchpoints:

  • Where does AI generate content or summaries?

  • Where does it classify, prioritise, triage, detect anomalies, or recommend actions?

  • Which artefacts (cases, orders, invoices, tickets) and which decisions are affected?

Write these down visually. Many teams discover that they haven’t fully mapped where AI is actually entering the process.

2. Clarify What Humans Do Now

For each touchpoint, define the human role in the new workflow:

  • Are they reviewing AI outputs?

  • Making final decisions?

  • Correcting errors or adding context?

  • Escalating edge cases?

  • “Teaching” the system through structured feedback?

Update RACI and handoffs to reflect hybrid tasks. Document who is accountable, who is responsible, who must be consulted, and who is informed when AI is in the loop.

Without this clarity, you get blind trust (“the system is always right”) or blanket distrust (“I ignore the system and do it myself”), and both damage value. Human accountability doesn’t disappear in AI workflows. It just becomes easier to blur if you don’t design for it.

 

3. Decide What Must Stay Human

Just because AI can influence a decision doesn’t mean it should own it.

Work with risk, compliance, and business stakeholders to define:

  • Decisions that are always human, regardless of model confidence.

  • Decisions where AI can propose but humans must sign off.

  • Low‑risk decisions that can be auto‑handled under defined thresholds.

Document human‑in‑the‑loop patterns like:

  • Approve‑before‑act – AI proposes, human approves, then the action occurs.

  • Approve‑after‑act – AI acts, humans review samples or exceptions.

  • Review‑on‑exception – humans only touch anomalies and risk‑tiered cases.

Set clear thresholds for auto‑accept and auto‑escalate. This is where explainability and confidence measures matter.

4. Build Frontline Feedback Loops

Assume your first version of the redesigned workflow will be imperfect. The question is whether you’ll learn from it quickly.

Design simple ways for frontline staff to report:

  • Quality issues – where AI outputs are consistently off.

  • Cognitive overload – steps where AI adds complexity.

  • Handoff problems – confusion about who owns a case or decision.

Establish review cadences with named owners. Close the loop: when feedback leads to changes in models or steps, communicate those changes back to the teams.

Apply this blueprint early in the programme and revisit it regularly. Don’t let workflow design freeze after go‑live.

 

Operating Model Redesign: The Hidden Layer

Behind every workflow sits an operating model: who owns processes, who makes decisions, how performance is measured.

AI changes these structures. Information becomes more widely available. Some decisions can be made closer to the frontline. Others move up because risk is higher. Some teams find their workload redistributed.

If you don’t make these changes explicit:

  • Authority and workload can shift in ways nobody has agreed.

  • Shadow decision‑makers emerge.

  • Tensions build as power dynamics change.

OCM’s role is to surface and manage this layer:

  • Make changes to ownership, decision rights, and accountabilities visible and communicated.

  • Involve stakeholders whose authority or workload shifts.

  • Help leaders anticipate cultural and power implications of the new design.

 

Where SAP Signavio and SAP Activate Support the Blueprint

In SAP‑enabled environments, two assets are particularly helpful.

  • SAP Signavio lets you model current processes, simulate AI‑driven changes, and connect outcomes to SAP execution reality. You can see where AI capabilities would sit, how they alter flows, and what impact they have on cycle times and handoffs.apty

  • SAP Activate includes an OCM workstream that brings people and process considerations into the project from Discover to Run, rather than bolting them on at the end.prosci

Use these tools as collaboration environments. Bring process owners, IT, and frontline representatives together to co‑design human–AI workflows. Don’t treat them as static documentation repositories.

 

A Concrete Scenario: Invoice Processing

Take invoice processing as an example.

Pre‑AI, the process might involve:

  • Manual capture and validation of invoice data.

  • Rule‑based checks for duplicates, coding, and thresholds.

  • Exceptions handled by experienced staff.

  • Approvals mainly driven by static limits.

Post‑AI, you may have:

  • Automated capture and validation.

  • Anomaly detection flagging unusual patterns.

  • Recommendation engines proposing approvals, routes, and actions.

Apply the blueprint:

  • Define anomaly classes that always require human review vs. those that can be auto‑handled, with confidence thresholds (for example, “over 95% confidence, under €X, auto‑approve”).

  • Clarify that analysts now focus on investigating flagged patterns rather than repeating machine checks.

  • Establish escalation routes and time‑to‑decision targets for ambiguous cases.

  • Feed false positives and negatives into model and rule refinement.

The result: faster cycle times, preserved or improved risk control, and clearer accountability – not just “AI in invoices”.

 

Design Assets and KPIs That Make It Stick

To keep this grounded, OCM can help deliver:

  • Human–AI RACI maps per process.

  • Decision matrices by risk tier and confidence threshold.

  • Escalation maps and response SLAs.

  • Exception‑handling playbooks.

  • Embedded guidance copy for SAP UX.

  • KPI packs tracking throughput, queue depth, exception rates, override patterns, rework, cognitive load signals, and model performance.

These assets let you see and manage the human–AI collaboration, not just the system status.

 

Pick One Workflow and Redesign It With AI in Mind

If AI is live but value feels elusive, don’t start with the tech. Start with a workflow.

Call to action:
Choose one end‑to‑end process where AI is already in play. Run the four‑step blueprint with the people who live that process every day – and let what you learn there guide the rest of your AI portfolio.
