entry.title: Bridging SAP Authorization Models & Databricks : An Architecture Pattern for Security Migration
entry.content:
Introduction

As organizations modernize their analytics platforms, migrating business data from SAP to modern Lakehouse architectures has become increasingly common. However, one critical aspect is often overlooked—security migration.

SAP's authorization model is built upon roles, authorization objects, organizational restrictions, and user-role assignments that collectively determine access to business data. Simply migrating the underlying data without preserving these access controls can lead to governance and compliance challenges.

Rather than directly exposing SAP security tables to downstream platforms, this article proposes a CDS View-based Security Semantic Layer that standardizes authorization metadata and provides a reusable foundation for enterprise security modernization initiatives.

In this article, we will explore the core SAP security tables involved in authorization management, design reusable CDS Views for metadata extraction, and architect a unified Security Semantic Layer for downstream consumers.

Figure 1: High-Level Security Metadata Extraction Architecture

High-Level Security Metadata Extraction Architecture

Understanding SAP Authorization Metadata

SAP's authorization framework is built upon multiple layers of security metadata that collectively determine a user's access to business data. Unlike traditional role-based access control models, SAP combines user-role assignments, authorization objects, authorization values, and organizational restrictions to enforce security policies.

At a high level, the authorization flow can be represented as follows:

Figure 2: SAP Authorization Metadata Flow

SAP Authorization Metadata Flow

The core SAP security tables involved in authorization management are:

SAP Table

Purpose

AGR_DEFINE

Stores role definitions and metadata

AGR_USERS

Stores user-to-role assignments

AGR_1251

Stores authorization objects, fields, and values

TOBJ

Stores authorization object metadata

Each table plays a specific role in defining who can access business data and under what conditions.

  • AGR_DEFINE acts as the role master table and contains information about SAP roles.
  • AGR_USERS maps SAP users to their assigned roles.
  • AGR_1251 contains the actual authorization rules, including authorization objects, field values, and organizational restrictions.
  • TOBJ provides semantic information about authorization objects used throughout the SAP authorization framework.

Together, these tables provide all the metadata required to design a reusable Security Semantic Layer for downstream analytics and governance platforms.

Why Only These Tables?

For most authorization migration and metadata extraction initiatives, these four tables provide the minimum and sufficient set of security metadata required to represent:

  • Role Definitions
  • User-to-Role Assignments
  • Authorization Rules
  • Organizational Restrictions
  • Authorization Object Metadata

By standardizing the information contained within these tables, organizations can create a business-friendly representation of SAP's authorization model without directly exposing low-level SAP security structures to downstream consumers.

Designing CDS Views for Security Extraction

Directly exposing SAP authorization tables to downstream platforms may appear to be the simplest approach for security migration. However, tightly coupling external consumers to SAP's underlying security tables introduces maintainability and extensibility challenges.

A better approach is to introduce a reusable Security Semantic Layer using CDS Views that abstracts SAP's authorization model into business-friendly entities.

Why CDS Views?

CDS Views provide several advantages when designing authorization extraction frameworks:

  • Semantic abstraction of SAP security metadata.
  • Centralized authorization metadata management.
  • Improved maintainability and extensibility.
  • Reduced dependency on underlying SAP table structures.
  • Reusability across multiple downstream consumers.

Instead of exposing raw SAP tables, CDS Views allow us to represent authorization metadata in a standardized and reusable format.

CDS View Architecture

The following architecture illustrates the proposed CDS View design.

Figure 3: CDS View Architecture 

CDS View Architecture

Proposed CDS Views

CDS View

Source Table

Purpose

ZI_ROLE_MASTER

AGR_DEFINE

Role metadata

ZI_ROLE_USER_MAPPING

AGR_USERS

User-to-role mapping

ZI_ROLE_AUTHORIZATION

AGR_1251

Authorization rules and restrictions

ZI_AUTHORIZATION_METADATA

TOBJ

Authorization object metadata

ZI_SAP_SECURITY_MODEL

Composite CDS View

Unified security model

Recommended Development Sequence

The CDS Views should be developed in the following order:

  1. ZI_ROLE_MASTER
  2. ZI_ROLE_USER_MAPPING
  3. ZI_ROLE_AUTHORIZATION
  4. ZI_AUTHORIZATION_METADATA
  5. ZI_SAP_SECURITY_MODEL

The Unified Security Model should consume all the previously created CDS Views and act as the canonical representation of SAP authorization metadata.

Unified Security Model

The final CDS View should expose a business-friendly representation of SAP's authorization framework.

Example:

By introducing a layered CDS View architecture, organizations can create a reusable and semantically rich Security Semantic Layer that simplifies authorization metadata extraction while remaining independent of any specific downstream technology.

Building the Security Semantic Layer

Once the CDS Views have been designed, the final step is to expose a Unified Security Model that can act as the single source of truth for SAP authorization metadata.

Rather than tightly coupling downstream systems to SAP's internal security tables, the Security Semantic Layer provides a standardized and reusable representation of roles, authorizations, and organizational restrictions.

Figure 4: Security Semantic Layer Architecture

Security Semantic Layer Architecture

The Security Semantic Layer enables organizations to:

  • Standardize authorization metadata.
  • Simplify downstream integrations.
  • Decouple analytics platforms from SAP's internal table structures.
  • Improve maintainability and extensibility of security metadata.

Since the Security Semantic Layer is technology agnostic, it can be leveraged by various analytics, governance, and modernization platforms without requiring changes to the underlying SAP authorization model.

5. Conclusion

Security migration should not be treated as an afterthought during enterprise analytics modernization initiatives. Authorization metadata is just as critical as business data and deserves the same level of architectural consideration.

By introducing a layered CDS View architecture and a reusable Security Semantic Layer, organizations can preserve SAP's mature authorization framework while simplifying future integrations with modern analytics and governance platforms.

Rather than exposing low-level SAP security tables directly, standardizing authorization metadata at the SAP layer provides a scalable, maintainable, and business-friendly foundation for enterprise security modernization initiatives.

entry.title: The InfoSet ZHR2_XJ_PA_ADHOC is not being generated in transaction SQ02.
entry.content:
During the InfoSet generation process, the system checks the TRDIR table for the following entry:

  • NAME = AQIO==ZHR2_XJ_PA_ADHOC
  • SUBC = 1

However, no corresponding entry exists in the TRDIR table for this combination. As a result, the generation process does not proceed further, and the InfoSet is not generated.

entry.title: Building Custom AI Agents with SAP Joule Studio: A Practical Guide Part-1
entry.content:
Introduction

Joule is already available in SAP Launchpad for many users, and it is great for broad questions.
My challenge started when business users asked for operational actions, not guidance.

A user asked:
“Show all blocked sales orders for customer 17100001 and remove the delivery block for order 487.”

I am unable to directly retrieve or display information on open sales orders due to system limitations. However, I can guide you on how to use the 'Sales Order - Query' to access this information manually. Would you like me to provide instructions on how to do this?

Out of the box, Joule gave a helpful explanation, but it did not execute the business action.
So I built a custom agent in Joule Studio that could read and update sales orders in SAP S/4HANA through OData APIs.

The Two Building Blocks: Skills & Agents

Everything in Joule Studio revolves around two core concepts:

1. Skill — A Specific Task

A  skill is a single, focused capability. Think of it as one action your agent can perform. It could be:

  1. A CRUD operation (Create, Read, Update, Delete)
  2. A process or automation
  3. A  condition check
  4. Launching another Joule skill
  5. Each skill does one thing well. For example:
    • Read all sales orders
    • Update delivery block on a sales order
    • Create a new sales order
    • Delete sales order.
2. Agent — The Orchestrator

An agent is the brain that holds multiple skills together. It decides when to use which skill based on what the user asks. An agent can even call other agents — making it possible to build complex, multi-step workflows.

Anatomy of an Agent
When you create an agent in Joule Studio, you'll work with several important configuration areas:

Expertise & Instructions

  1. Expertise — Tell the agent what domain it specializes in. Is it for HR? Sales? Procurement? This helps the agent understand its boundaries.
  2. Instructions — This is where the magic happens. You're essentially writing a playbook for the agent:
    1. What to do and what not to do
    2. How to format responses
    3. When to trigger which skill
    4. Rules and guardrail

Additional Context — Common abbreviations, company-specific terminology, or any contextual information the agent might need to understand your business language.

Model Settings
You get to choose your LLM provider:

  • Anthropic
  • OpenAI
  • Google
  • MistralAI

Enable Backup LLM Provider. This sets up automatic failover — if your primary provider is slow or unavailable, the backup kicks in automatically, keeping your agent running without interruption.

MCP Servers
Add MCP (Model Context Protocol) Servers to give your agent access to additional tools and external capabilities.

Tools
Add tools that provide the agent with additional context, system data access, or the ability to perform actions.

  • calculator
  • joule skill
  • joule agent
  • Documents(Document Grounding)

Agent Output

Define how the output should be formatted:

  1. Markdown
  2. Text
  3. Data Type
  4. JSON
What I Built (Actual Implementation)

I implemented a Sales Order Assistant agent for a finance and sales operations scenario.

Scope of the agent:

  • Read sales orders with filters (customer, status, billing date, block flags)
  • Update delivery block on a specific sales order
  • Return a table-style summary for business users
  • Refuse to answer if live data cannot be read

Technical stack:

  • Joule Studio agent
  • 3 custom skills (GET, PATCH, POST)
  • SAP S/4HANA OData service: API_SALES_ORDER_SRV
  • Destination-based connectivity in test environment
  • Primary + backup LLM provider enabled
Step 1: Skills I Created

I separated skills by operation so tool selection remained predictable.

1. Read Sales Orders (GET)
Purpose: Retrieve sales orders and basic status fields.

2. Modify Sales Order (PATCH)
Purpose: Update delivery block and selected editable fields.

3. Create Sales Order (POST)
Purpose: Create a new order for approved scenarios.

Why this helped:
When I initially combined read and update into one “do everything” skill, the agent often chose the wrong operation. Splitting skills reduced tool-calling errors significantly.

Step 2: Agent Instructions I Actually Used

The single biggest improvement came from strict instructions.

Key rules I configured:

1. Always call Read Sales Orders before any status answer.
2. Call Modify Sales Order only when user intent is clearly an update.
3. Never generate order numbers, dates, or statuses without API output.
4. On 4XX/5XX, return a clear business-safe error message and next step.

Response format:

  • Always output a compact table for list queries.
  • Always include order ID, customer, billing date, and status.
  • Flag risky records: overdue billing, missing assignment, missing reference.

What changed after this:
Before strict rules, I saw occasional “plausible” but unverified responses. After adding mandatory tool-use rules, those stopped.

Step 3: Real Test Cases and Results

I tested using business-like prompts.

Test prompts:

  • “Show all blocked sales orders for customer 17100001.”
  • “Remove delivery block on order 500001234.”
  • “List overdue orders with missing assignment.”

Observed behavior:

  • Read queries: agent called GET skill, then summarized correctly.
  • Update queries: agent called PATCH skill with target order.
  • Failure case (invalid order): agent returned API error context and suggested verification steps.

One practical lesson:
If the API payload is too large, response quality drops. I reduced returned fields to only what users need and got more stable answers.

These were the most useful implementation learnings :

1. Keep skills narrow and explicit
Broad “multi-action” skills increase wrong tool selection.

2. Put tool-calling rules at the top of instructions
Priority placement improved consistency.

3. Add business-safe refusal behavior
If data retrieval fails, the agent must refuse to guess.

4. Minimize API response payload early
Too many fields create noisy reasoning and weaker summaries.

5. Add backup LLM provider, but validate output style
Failover worked, but I had to align formatting instructions across models.

6. Test with ambiguous prompts
Examples like “fix this order” forced me to add clarification logic before updates.

Execution Trace: What Happens Under the Hood (From My Test Runs)
For a prompt like “Show blocked orders for customer 17100001”:

1. Agent starts and loads instructions/context
2. Intent classified as data retrieval
3. GET skill called with filters
4. Raw response compressed
5. Agent applies formatting + risk flags
6. Final response returned as table

For an update prompt:

1. Agent detects action intent
2. Validates target order context via read
3. PATCH skill executes update
4. Agent confirms changed field and status

Real-World Example: Sales Order Assistant
Let me walk you through a practical example. My requirement was to build an agent that can:

  1. Handle natural language queries like *"Show all open orders for customer X"* or *"Remove delivery block on order Y"*
  2. Provide contextual insights (customer credit status, expected delivery dates)
  3. Suggest corrective actions and guide users through SAP Fiori apps

Step 1: Pick Your API

I went to the SAP Business Accelerator Hub and chose the standard OData service: `API_SALES_ORDER_SRV/`

Step 2: Create Skills

I built three separate skills — one for each operation:

Skill Operation Purpose
Read Sales OrdersGET Fetch all sales orders
Update Sales OrderPATCH Modify fields like delivery block
Create Sales OrderPOSTCreate a new sales order


The GET skill is that it retrieves all sales orders, but the agent processes the response based on your question. Ask for a specific customer? It filters and gives you only that data — not the entire list.

Step 3: Configure the Agent

Here's an example of how I set up the expertise and instructions:

Expertise:

You are an SAP Sales Order Assistant. Your expertise includes:
- Retrieving sales order details from SAP S/4HANA using OData services
- Explaining order status, delivery dates, and pricing information
- Helping users understand delivery blocks and billing workflows

Instructions:
CRITICAL RULES:
1. You MUST ALWAYS call the "Read Sales Order" skill before answering
ANY question about sales orders. NEVER answer from your own knowledge.
2. You MUST call "Modify Sales Order" skill when user tries to modify
a sales order.
3. If you cannot call the tool, inform the user that you need to
retrieve the data and cannot answer without it.

RESPONSIBILITIES:
- Retrieve sales order headers (order number, customer, billing date, status)
- Summarize order volumes by customer group, date range, or status
- Identify orders requiring attention (overdue billing, missing assignments)
- Present results in a clear table format

RESPONSE FORMAT:
| # | Sales Order | Customer Group | Billing Date | Status |
|---|------------|----------------|--------------|--------|
| 1 | 4500001234 | Group 1 | 2026-05-25 | Open |

DO NOT:
- Guess or fabricate order data
- Ignore Error 4XX responses — always inform the user

 

This is a basic example — in a real scenario, you'd provide far more detailed instructions based on your specific business requirements.

Additional Context

FIELD DEFINITIONS:
- Reference → Order reference / external document number
- Customer Group 1-4 → Classification groupings for the customer
- Assignment → Cost assignment or project reference
- Billing Date → Planned billing/invoicing date
- Exchng. Rate Accntg. → Exchange rate used for accounting
- Addit. Value Date → Additional value date for payment terms

SORTING & PRIORITY RULES:
- Default sort: Billing Date (earliest first)
- Highlight orders where Billing Date < today (overdue)
- Flag orders with empty Assignment or Reference fields

ERROR HANDLING:
- If API returns Error 4XX → inform user, suggest checking
authorization or filters

ORDER STATUS LOGIC:
- Billing Date < today + no invoice = "Overdue"
- Assignment field empty = "Needs Attention"
- Reference field empty = "Missing Reference"

Step 4: Connect Skills to Agent

The connection is straightforward:

Agent → Tools → Add Skill → Select your skill

Do this for each skill. Then, in your instructions, specify when the agent should trigger which skill.

Step 5: Test

Click Test, select your destination for the action calls, select your private environment, and start chatting:

  1. Give me sales order 987
  2. Show me all blocked sales orders
  3. Remove the delivery block on order 456

What Happens Under the Hood?

When you test your agent, you can see the internal execution trace. Here's what happens when a user asks something:

User Message

 

Breaking It Down:

PhaseWhat It Does
StartInitializes the agent, loads context, expertise, and instructions
Direct PromptingChecks if the request can be answered without complex reasoning
Decomposition Breaks the request into smaller sub-tasks
Plan Creates an execution plan — decides order of steps, which APIs to call
Agent Thinking The LLM reasons about the plan and decides the action to take
ReadExecutes the API call to S/4HANA
CompressorSummarizes the raw API response to stay within token limits
Agent Thinking (2nd)Processes compressed data and formulates the answer
Final Answer

Produces the user-facing response

Refinement

Quality check for accuracy, formatting, and completeness

End

Execution completes

Conclusion

The future of enterprise AI isn't just about having an AI assistant. It's about having your AI assistant, built for your business.

Joule gives strong baseline assistance, but business operations need domain-specific actions.
Joule Studio is where that becomes practical: you define the tools, the guardrails, and the behavior.

My key takeaway after implementation:
Good agent quality is less about which model and more about:

  • strict tool-calling instructions,
  • small and clean payloads,
  • and real-world test prompts with edge cases.

If you are building your first Joule Studio agent, start with one business process, 2-3 narrow skills, and strict non-hallucination rules. You will get better results faster than trying to build a broad agent on day one.

Remember: you're essentially teaching a new team member how to do their job. The clearer your instructions, the better the results. Once everything is working as expected — launch it, and let your custom agent work for your users.

entry.title: Re: API-Centric Integration on SAP Integration Suite – Part 1: Build and Deploy Your API
entry.content:
Hi @tiestvangool ,

You can create an API either via providing URL or via refering to a Destination created in the BTP cockpit. While creating a HTTP destination make sure you have added a label called "IntegrationCell.Include=true". I hope this clarifies.

https://help.sap.com/docs/integration-suite/isuite-integrations-and-apis/create-api-artifact-by-referring-to-destination?locale=en-US&version=CLOUD&ai=true

entry.title: Re: SAP SuccessFactors HCM: Updated Home Page 2H 2025
entry.content:
Hi @Liz_Moore1 ,

I think this was recently explained here: Updated Home Page – 1H Release Highlights - SAP Community

The Quick Actions use color attributes of the Portlet and Secondary Button entity and the Quick Links use color attributes of the Secondary Button entity. Secondard Button color attributes can only be changed via export/import of the theme.

BR
Sebastian

entry.title: 🐍 July Developer Challenge Week 3 — Multiplayer & Events
entry.content:
Week 3 — Multiplayer & Events

Challenge series: Snake & Ladder Developer Challenge | Week 3 of 4 Dates: 20–26 July 2026

The Setup

A single-player Snake & Ladder game is a solitaire puzzle. The real game is multiplayer — taking turns, watching opponents climb ladders, celebrating when a snake bites someone ahead of you.

This week you add a second player, implement turn rotation, and wire CAP events so the game state advances automatically. You'll also use the CAP MCP server to let an AI assistant watch the game — describing what happened on each turn in natural language.

What You'll Build This Week

  • Turn rotation: confirmMove activates the next player automatically
  • A GameSession state machine (separate from Player status)
  • A CAP event subscriber that drives turn handoff
  • A TurnLog entity recording every move for replay
  • CAP MCP server connected to the game — so an AI can query board state and narrate moves

Background: CAP Events as the Turn Engine

CAP events let services communicate without tight coupling. Your GameService emits a BoardEvent when a player moves. A subscriber — in the same service or a separate one — reacts to that event and advances the game.

CAP messaging key facts :

  • this.emit(eventName, data) inside an action handler automatically fires after the DB transaction commits — no manual outbox needed for in-process events.
  • This week uses in-process eventing (same cds watch process). If you later split into microservices, add file-based-messaging to package.json and your handler code stays unchanged — only the config changes.

This is the architecture for multiplayer:

Player calls rollDice
  → CAP validates @from: [#Playing]
  → Handler runs: calculates new position, emits BoardEvent
  → CAP applies @to: #Moving
  → Player calls confirmMove
  → CAP validates @from: [#Moving]
  → Handler emits TurnComplete event
  → CAP applies @to: #Waiting
  → TurnComplete subscriber: logs the turn (next-player activation already done inside confirmMove)

No polling. No setInterval. The event graph drives the game.

Step-by-Step

0. Upgrade to the latest CAP version

Before adding Week 3 features, upgrade your project to the latest @SAP/cds. CAP 10 brings performance improvements, refined flow-status behaviour, and updated MCP support.

If you skipped Week 2's CDS 10 note: cds init --nodejs in CDS 10 generates "type": "module" in package.json. The handler code in these guides uses CommonJS require(). If your server crashes with ReferenceError: require is not defined, remove "type": "module" from package.json. Running npm install @sap/cds@latest also removes it automatically.

npm install @sap/cds@latest @cap-js/mcp@latest @cap-js/sqlite@latest

Verify the versions installed:

cds --version

You should see @SAP/cds at 10.x or higher. Restart cds watch — if the service starts cleanly, you're ready.

Global CLI: The cds command comes from @sap/cds-dk. Keep it in sync:

npm install -g @sap/cds-dk@latest

1. Add the TurnLog entity

Add to db/schema.cds:

// ── Turn log — full history of every move ─────────────────────────────────
entity TurnLog {
  key ID          : UUID;
      session     : Association to GameSessions;
      player      : Association to Players;
      turnNumber  : Integer;
      diceRoll    : Integer;
      fromSquare  : Integer;
      toSquare    : Integer;
      eventType   : String(20);   // 'normal' | 'ladder' | 'snake' | 'doubleSnake' | 'win' | 'blocked'
      timestamp   : Timestamp;
}

Also update the Players entity in db/schema.cds to add these two fields needed by the confirmMove handler:

prevPosition  : Integer default 1;    // position before last dice roll
lastEventType : String(20);           // event from last roll: normal|ladder|snake|doubleSnake

Add it to srv/game-service.cds — inside the service GameService { } block, before the closing brace:

entity TurnLog as projection on snakeladder.TurnLog;

And add the TurnComplete event — also inside the service block so it appears in OData $metadata and the MCP describe tool:

event TurnComplete {
  sessionID    : UUID;
  playerID     : UUID;
  nextPlayerID : UUID;
  turnNumber   : Integer;
}

2. Implement turn rotation in the handler

Update srv/game-service.js — replace confirmMove and add the TurnComplete subscriber:

Before implementing confirmMove: Your Week 2 rollDice handler must store two additional fields during the UPDATE(Players) call. If you haven't already, add these to the .set({...}) block in rollDice:

prevPosition: player.position,     // position BEFORE the move (used for TurnLog)
lastEventType: event?.type ?? 'normal'  // ladder/snake/doubleSnake/normal

These are read by confirmMove when writing the TurnLog entry.

// ── confirmMove ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
// After this returns, CAP sets status → #Waiting.
// We then find the next player and set them to #Playing.
this.on('confirmMove', 'Players', async (req) => {
  // Players and TurnLog are already destructured at the top of init()
  const player = await SELECT.one.from(Players).where({ ID: req.params[0].ID })
  if (!player) return req.error(404, 'Player not found')

  // ── Check win condition before handing off ──
  if (player.position === 100) {
    // Delegate to winGame — correct CAP bound-action call syntax
    await this.send({ event: 'winGame', entity: 'Players', params: [{ ID: player.ID }] })
    return
  }

  // ── Log this turn ──
  const [{ n: count }] = await SELECT`count(*) as n`.from(TurnLog)
    .where({ session_ID: player.session_ID })
  const turnNumber = (count || 0) + 1
  await INSERT.into(TurnLog).entries({
    ID: cds.utils.uuid(),
    session_ID: player.session_ID,
    player_ID: player.ID,
    turnNumber,
    diceRoll: player.lastRoll,
    fromSquare: player.prevPosition,            // pre-roll position stored by rollDice
    toSquare: player.position,
    eventType: player.lastEventType ?? 'normal', // event type stored by rollDice
    timestamp: new Date().toISOString()
  })

  // ── Find next player in turn order ──
  const players = await SELECT.from(Players)
    .where({ session_ID: player.session_ID })
    .orderBy('turnOrder')

  const idx = players.findIndex(p => p.ID === player.ID)
  // Skip finished players — guard counter prevents infinite loop if all finish simultaneously
  let nextIdx = (idx + 1) % players.length
  let guard = 0
  while (players[nextIdx].TurnStatus === 'Finished' && guard++ < players.length) {
    nextIdx = (nextIdx + 1) % players.length
  }
  const next = players[nextIdx]

  await this.emit('TurnComplete', {
    sessionID: player.session_ID,
    playerID: player.ID,
    nextPlayerID: next.ID,
    turnNumber
  })

  // Activate next player — use direct UPDATE, not the flow action,
  // because we're already inside a handler
  await UPDATE(Players)
    .set({ TurnStatus: 'Playing' })
    .where({ ID: next.ID })
})

// ── TurnComplete event subscriber ─────────────────────────────────────────
// NOTE: this.after() on custom events receives (handlerResult, req) — the
// first argument is undefined (no return value from the event). Use req.data.
this.after('TurnComplete', async (_results, req) => {
  const data = req.data
  console.log(`[TurnComplete] session=${data.sessionID} nextPlayer=${data.nextPlayerID} turn=${data.turnNumber}`)
})

3. Add a session-level startGame action

Players need a way to transition a session from "lobby" to "in progress". Add to db/schema.cds:

type SessionStatus : String enum {
  Lobby;      // Accepting players, not yet started
  InProgress; // Game is running
  Complete;   // A player has won
}

@flow.status: SessionStatus
entity GameSessions {
  key ID          : UUID;
      name        : String(100);
      @cds.on.insert: $now
      createdAt   : Timestamp;
      startedAt   : Timestamp;
      finishedAt  : Timestamp;
      winner      : Association to Players;
      @readonly
      SessionStatus : SessionStatus default #Lobby;
}

Add to srv/game-service.cds actions on GameSessions:

@flow.status: SessionStatus
entity GameSessions as projection on snakeladder.GameSessions actions {

  @from: [ #Lobby ]
  @to: #InProgress
  action startGame();

  @from: [ #InProgress ]
  @to: #Complete
  action endGame();
}

Also verify startTurn is still in your Players actions from Week 2. If you rebuilt your service file, make sure it includes:

@from: [ #Waiting ]
@to: #Playing
action startTurn();

In the multiplayer flow, startGame sets the first player directly to Playing, and confirmMove advances turns via direct UPDATE — so startTurn is not strictly required for multiplayer turn rotation. However, it's still useful for manual testing and for single-player Week 2 scenarios. Keep it in your service definition.

Implement startGame in game-service.js:

// Players, BoardSquares, TurnLog are already destructured at the top of init()
// Make sure your top-of-init destructure includes ALL three:
// const { Players, BoardSquares, TurnLog } = this.entities
this.on('startGame', 'GameSessions', async (req) => {
  const sessionID = req.params[0].ID

  // All players start in Waiting — set the first in turn order to Playing
  const players = await SELECT.from(Players)
    .where({ session_ID: sessionID })
    .orderBy('turnOrder')

  if (players.length < 2) {
    return req.reject(400, 'A game needs at least 2 players to start.')
  }

  await UPDATE(Players)
    .set({ TurnStatus: 'Playing' })
    .where({ ID: players[0].ID })

  await UPDATE('snakeladder.GameSessions')
    .set({ startedAt: new Date().toISOString() })
    .where({ ID: sessionID })
})

Also add the endGame handler. This action is triggered by the @flow.status machinery (it transitions SessionStatus to #Complete automatically), but it must also set finishedAt:

this.on('endGame', 'GameSessions', async (req) => {
  await UPDATE('snakeladder.GameSessions')
    .set({ finishedAt: new Date().toISOString() })
    .where({ ID: req.params[0].ID })
})

4. Test multiplayer turn rotation

Create a session with two players and start the game:

Tip: Omit the ID field in POST bodies — CAP auto-generates a UUID. Copy the returned ID from each response to use in subsequent requests.

POST http://localhost:4004/odata/v4/game/GameSessions
Content-Type: application/json
{
  "name": "My First Multiplayer Game"
}
POST http://localhost:4004/odata/v4/game/Players
Content-Type: application/json
{
  "session_ID": "<session-ID-from-above>",
  "name": "Alice",
  "turnOrder": 1
}
POST http://localhost:4004/odata/v4/game/Players
Content-Type: application/json
{
  "session_ID": "<session-ID-from-above>",
  "name": "Bob",
  "turnOrder": 2
}
POST http://localhost:4004/odata/v4/game/GameSessions(<session-ID>)/startGame
Content-Type: application/json
{}

Now Alice should be #Playing and Bob should be #Waiting. Roll for Alice, confirm her move, and Bob should automatically become #Playing.

Note on service path: CAP maps GameService to /odata/v4/game. Replace GameService in all URLs with game. See the Week 1 note for details.

Verify by checking both players' statuses after confirmMove:

GET http://localhost:4004/odata/v4/game/Players?$filter=session_ID eq '<session-ID>'

5. Connect the CAP MCP Server

The CAP MCP server exposes your GameService as tools that any MCP-compatible AI assistant (Claude Code, Claude Desktop, Opencode) can call. The AI can query board state, roll dice, narrate turns — all via natural language.

Install

npm add @cap-js/mcp

That's it. No extra configuration needed. @cap-js/mcp auto-detects CAP services during cds watch.

Annotate GameService

Add @McP to your service declaration in srv/game-service.cds, plus @title and @description on entities and actions so the AI understands the game domain:

This shows only the annotations to add — keep your existing action definitions unchanged. The // ... other actions placeholder represents your existing rollDice, confirmMove, etc.

/**
 * Snake & Ladder Game Service — multiplayer board game engine.
 * Players take turns rolling dice and moving tokens across a 10×10 board.
 * Snakes send players back; ladders advance them. First to reach square 100 wins.
 */
@mcp
@protocol: ['odata-v4', 'mcp']   // keep OData alongside MCP — REQUIRED or OData drops
@title: 'Snake & Ladder Game'
service GameService {

  @title: 'Game Sessions'
  @description: 'Create a session, add players, then call startGame. SessionStatus: Lobby → InProgress → Complete.'
  @flow.status: SessionStatus
  entity GameSessions as projection on snakeladder.GameSessions actions {
    /** Start the game. Requires at least 2 players. Sets first player (turnOrder=1) to Playing. */
    @from: [ #Lobby ] @to: #InProgress
    action startGame();
    // ... other actions
  }

  @title: 'Players'
  @description: 'Each player has a position (1-100) and TurnStatus. Call rollDice when Playing, then confirmMove to end the turn.'
  @flow.status: TurnStatus
  @cds.redirection.target
  entity Players as projection on snakeladder.Players actions {
    /**
     * Roll a 6-sided dice and move the player.
     * Returns: roll (1-6), position (new square after snakes/ladders), event (normal/ladder/snake/doubleSnake).
     */
    @from: [ #Playing ] @to: #Moving
    action rollDice() returns { roll: Integer; position: Integer; event: String; };
    // ... other actions
  }

  @title: 'Board State'
  @description: 'Live view of all player positions. Filter by session_ID to see a specific game.'
  @readonly
  entity BoardState as select from snakeladder.Players {
    key ID,
    name, position, TurnStatus, lastRoll,
    session.ID   as session_ID : UUID,   // typed alias — makes session_ID OData-filterable
    session.name as sessionName
  };
}

Start cds watch and the MCP endpoint appears automatically

cds watch

Look for this line in the output:

[cds] - serving GameService {
  at: [ '/odata/v4/game', '/mcp/game' ],
  ...
}

The MCP server is live at http://localhost:4004/mcp/game.

Add MCP-callable wrapper functions

The @cap-js/mcp query tool reads data; bound actions on entities (like rollDice) aren't directly callable by the AI. Add unbound service functions that the AI can call via per_action_tool. Add these to srv/game-service.cds inside the service block:

// ── MCP-callable unbound functions — allow AI to drive the game ──────────
/** Start a game session. Requires sessionID of a Lobby session with ≥2 players. */
function mcpStartGame(sessionID: UUID) returns String;

/** Roll the dice for the current Playing player. */
function mcpRollDice(playerID: UUID) returns { roll: Integer; position: Integer; event: String; };

/** Confirm a move and rotate the turn to the next player. */
function mcpConfirmMove(playerID: UUID) returns String;

/** Get the current board state for a game session. */
function mcpBoardState(sessionID: UUID) returns array of {
  name: String; position: Integer; TurnStatus: String; lastRoll: Integer;
};

/** All 100 squares with snake/ladder info. */
function mcpBoardMap() returns array of {
  square: Integer; snakeTo: Integer; ladderTo: Integer; isDoubleHead: Boolean;
};

Implement them in srv/game-service.js (add before return super.init()😞

Critical: use the object form of this.send() for bound entity actions. The string form this.send('rollDice', { params }) puts { params } into req.data of the dispatched request — bound action handlers read the entity key from req.params[0].ID, which will be undefined. Your handlers will run silently returning success but touching nothing. Always use the object form:

// ✗ WRONG — params land in req.data, req.params is empty
await this.send('rollDice', { params: [{ ID: playerID }] })

// ✓ CORRECT — params routed to req.params[0]
await this.send({ event: 'rollDice', entity: 'Players', params: [{ ID: playerID }] })
this.on('mcpStartGame', async (req) => {
  // Use object form: string form puts params in req.data, not req.params
  await this.send({ event: 'startGame', entity: 'GameSessions', params: [{ ID: req.data.sessionID }] })
  return `Game started for session ${req.data.sessionID}`
})
this.on('mcpRollDice', async (req) => {
  return await this.send({ event: 'rollDice', entity: 'Players', params: [{ ID: req.data.playerID }] })
})
this.on('mcpConfirmMove', async (req) => {
  await this.send({ event: 'confirmMove', entity: 'Players', params: [{ ID: req.data.playerID }] })
  return `Move confirmed for player ${req.data.playerID}`
})
this.on('mcpBoardState', async (req) => {
  return await SELECT.from(Players).where({ session_ID: req.data.sessionID })
    .columns('name','position','TurnStatus','lastRoll').orderBy('turnOrder')
})
this.on('mcpBoardMap', async () => SELECT.from(BoardSquares).orderBy('square'))

Enable per_action_tool in package.json:

{
  "cds": {
    "mcp": { "per_action_tool": true }
  }
}

After restarting, the MCP server exposes 7 tools: describe, query, mcpStartGame, mcpRollDice, mcpConfirmMove, mcpBoardState, mcpBoardMap.

 

Note: You can use any MCP tool of your choice. 

Use with Claude Code (auto-wired — just ask)

With cds watch running, open a new terminal and ask Claude Code directly:

claude "What is the current board state? Who is in the lead?"

claude "Show me the turn history for the most recent game session."

claude "Which squares have snakes and which have ladders? Summarise the dangerous zones."

Claude Code uses the query and describe MCP tools to read your live game data and answer.

Use with Claude Desktop (manual wiring)

If you want the AI to also roll dice and make moves, connect Claude Desktop. Add to claude_desktop_config.json (macOS: ~/Library/Application Support/Claude/claude_desktop_config.json😞

{
  "mcpServers": {
    "snake-ladder": {
      "command": "npx",
      "args": ["-y", "mcp-remote", "http://localhost:4004/mcp/game"]
    }
  }
}

Restart Claude Desktop, then try:

"What is the current board state for the active game? Who is winning?"

"Roll the dice for the current Playing player and tell me what happened."

"Narrate the last 5 turns like a sports commentator."

Verify the MCP endpoint — tools list and action tests

Step 1 — List all tools (should be exactly 7):

curl -X POST http://localhost:4004/mcp/game \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "Accept: application/json, text/event-stream" \
  -d '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":1,"method":"tools/list","params":{}}'

Expected: SSE response with tools array containing query, describe, mcpStartGame, mcpRollDice, mcpConfirmMove, mcpBoardState, mcpBoardMap.

Step 2 — mcpBoardMap (no session needed):

curl -X POST http://localhost:4004/mcp/game \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "Accept: application/json, text/event-stream" \
  -d '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":2,"method":"tools/call","params":{"name":"mcpBoardMap","arguments":{}}}'

Expected: 100 squares with snakeTo, ladderTo, isDoubleHead fields.

Step 3 — mcpStartGame (use an existing Lobby session ID):

curl -X POST http://localhost:4004/mcp/game \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "Accept: application/json, text/event-stream" \
  -d '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":3,"method":"tools/call","params":{"name":"mcpStartGame","arguments":{"sessionID":"<your-session-id>"}}}'

Expected response: "result": "Game started for session ...". Then verify with OData:

GET http://localhost:4004/odata/v4/game/GameSessions(<session-id>)?$select=SessionStatus,startedAt

SessionStatus should be "InProgress" and startedAt not null.

If mcpStartGame returns success but SessionStatus stays Lobby, you have the this.send() bug — the string form was used instead of the object form. See the critical note above.

Step 4 — mcpRollDice (use the Playing player's ID):

curl -X POST http://localhost:4004/mcp/game \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "Accept: application/json, text/event-stream" \
  -d '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":4,"method":"tools/call","params":{"name":"mcpRollDice","arguments":{"playerID":"<alice-id>"}}}'

Expected: {"roll":N, "position":M, "event":"normal|ladder|snake|doubleSnake"}. If you get "result": null, the this.send() object form is not being used.

Step 5 — mcpConfirmMove:

curl -X POST http://localhost:4004/mcp/game \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "Accept: application/json, text/event-stream" \
  -d '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":5,"method":"tools/call","params":{"name":"mcpConfirmMove","arguments":{"playerID":"<alice-id>"}}}'

Expected: "result": "Move confirmed for player ...". Check Bob's TurnStatus is now "Playing".

Step 6 — mcpBoardState:

curl -X POST http://localhost:4004/mcp/game \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -H "Accept: application/json, text/event-stream" \
  -d '{"jsonrpc":"2.0","id":6,"method":"tools/call","params":{"name":"mcpBoardState","arguments":{"sessionID":"<your-session-id>"}}}'

Expected: array of players with name, position, TurnStatus, lastRoll.

MCP uses SSE transport — responses arrive as event: message\ndata: {...}. Strip the data: prefix to parse JSON. HTTP clients that don't support SSE (like some REST clients) will show the raw text.

Understanding the Two State Machines

After this week you have two independent status flows running in your service:

Entity Field States Purpose

GameSessionsSessionStatusLobby → InProgress → CompleteGoverns the game lifecycle
PlayersTurnStatusWaiting → Playing → Moving → Blocked / FinishedGoverns each player's turn

The session state machine controls whether the game is happening. The player state machine controls whose turn it is and what they can do. CAP enforces both independently with their own @flow.status annotations.

Stretch Goal

Add a skipTurn action that a blocked player can call as an alternative to unblockPlayer, which also records how many turns they've been blocked:

@from: [ #Blocked ]
@to: $flow.previous
action skipTurn();
this.on('skipTurn', 'Players', async (req) => {
  const player = await SELECT.one.from(Players).where({ ID: req.params[0].ID })
  await UPDATE(Players)
    .set({ turnsBlocked: (player.turnsBlocked || 0) + 1 })
    .where({ ID: player.ID })
})

Note: BoardState is already included as a required entity in Step 5 (inside the @mcp-annotated service). The @cds.on.insert: $now annotation on GameSessions.createdAt coexists correctly with the Week 1 seed CSV — CAP uses the annotation for programmatic inserts but still honours explicit values in seed files.

How to Submit

Reply to the Week 3 blog post on SAP Community with a comment containing:

  1. A screenshot or JSON response showing two players after confirmMove — one Waiting, one Playing
  2. A screenshot or curl response showing mcpRollDice returning {roll, position, event} (not null) — proves the MCP action tools are wired correctly
  3. A screenshot of Claude Code or Claude Desktop ( or any MCP tool client of your choice) querying your game state via MCP — even a simple "who is winning?" response counts
  4. Optional but celebrated: a short AI-narrated turn log using mcpBoardState + TurnLog

What's Next

In Week 4 — Fiori UI & CAP MCP, you wire up the ready-made board UI, add your service URL to see your live game on a real Snake & Ladder board to complete the challenge.

Resources

  • CAP Status Flows Documentation
  • CAP MCP Server
  • CAP Node.js Events

entry.title: Chart of Depreciation created with Reference and can see Depreciation areas copied
entry.content: New Chart of Depreciation created with Reference(Which has only 01,15,32 Dep.areas) and can see Depreciation areas automatically copied with 1,15,31,32,33 in table T093A, but not in Table T093. I manually created dep.areas 34 &35 with IFRS Accounting principle and this is causing issue while creating the Chart of depreciation area assignment to company code it shows the error as "Parallel crcy area (crcy type 30) missing for area 34 ch. of deprec. XXXX". How to fix it and how to assign the company code to chart of depreciation.

entry.title: Re: The refreshed SAP Integration Certification Framework is now available
entry.content: Exciting news for SAP Partners. Thanks Shmila for sharing.

entry.title: Re: Route Determination for One-Time Customers in S/4HANA...
entry.content:
Hello @Manish_12 

Transportation zone is part of one-time address. In other words it can be provided during document creation like any other part of the address e.g. street, country etc. The route determination should use that transportation zone, not the one provided in one-time partner master data.

Doesn't it work this way?

Best regards

Dominik Tylczynski

entry.title: Re: Parallel Processing in ABAP using SPTA Framework
entry.content:
Hi @Sandra_Rossi ,

Thanks for your valuable insight. I completely agree that CL_ABAP_PARALLEL and bgPF are the modern, officially supported frameworks for implementing parallel processing in newer ABAP environments, especially on SAP S/4HANA and ABAP Cloud.

My intention in discussing the SPTA framework was primarily from the perspective of understanding and maintaining legacy applications, where it is still encountered. It's important for ABAP developers working on existing ECC and older S/4HANA systems to be familiar with it, even though it is no longer the recommended approach for new developments.

I appreciate you highlighting the newer alternatives and sharing the SAP Community resource. It provides useful guidance for developers choosing the right parallel processing framework today.

entry.title: SAP Sales & Service Cloud Version 2: SCIM API based Automatic User Provisioning
entry.content:
Very recently as SAP Introduced SCIM(System for Cross-domain Identity Management) API for SAP Sales & Service Cloud version 2. SCIM. SCIM is not an SAP only concept, rather it is a industry wide open standard/protocol that automates the creation, updating, and deletion of user accounts(along with their authorisation) across SAP/non-SAP based applications.

I was recently challenged by my customer to enable the automatic user synchronization from their corporate identity provider(in this case, Microsoft EntraID). So without any further "jargon", lets straight dive into the steps.

 

Systems Involved:

  • SAP Sales & Service Cloud Version 2(SSC V2)
  • Corporate Identity Provide(Microsoft EntraID)
  • SAP Identity Provisioning Service(SAP IPS)

Note: In my scenario, there was no SAP Identity Authentication Service(SAP IAS) involved, not even as a proxy.  But even if SAP IAS is involved, most setup steps remains the same except for change about which is your source system(depending on if SAP IAS is in proxy mode or SAP IAS is also identity provider).

Watchout: To execute these setup steps, you will need the Administrator Privilege on ALL of the mentioned system above. I assume that either you have them OR you know someone who can execute these steps on your behalf in the respective systems.

 

References/Documentations to get started:

As always, this blog is just to share my experience with the implementation. Hence SAP official documents still remains your "single source of truth" 😊,

https://help.sap.com/docs/CX_NG_SVC/86460d5a012440698a48c67a669d9730/9fc2d026a6264806895047a2033e411f.html?locale=en-US --> Official Integration Guide

https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-identity-services/cloud-identity-services/target-sap-sales-cloud-and-sap-service-cloud?locale=en-US --> to set SSC V2 as Target System

https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-identity-services/cloud-identity-services/microsoft-entra-id?locale=en-US --> to set EntraID as Source System

https://community.sap.com/t5/crm-and-cx-blog-posts-by-sap/introducing-scim-api-for-sap-sales-amp-service-cloud-v2-seamless-user-sync/ba-p/14200658 --> Good to know about, what is SCIM API and how to use it

https://community.sap.com/t5/technology-blog-posts-by-sap/provision-users-from-microsoft-entraid-to-sap-cloud-identity-services/ba-p/13546054 --> to follow the EntraID Configuration steps

 

Steps:

SAP Sales and Service Cloud Version 2

Setup in SSC V2 is on the lighter side. You just need to configure a Technical User as well as it permission(aka Technical Role). 

  • Under "System Settings" --> "Users and Control" --> Roles, Create a technical role. Dont forget to set it as "Active" 😜.

 

  • Assign the respective role authorisations/services.

 

  • Under "System Settings" --> "Users and Control" --> Users, create a technical user. Again, dont forget to ensure that it as "Active" 😜.

  • And assign this user the technical role created above.

 Thats all for the SSC V2 side of setup steps.

 

Microsoft EntraID

As these steps are pretty standard for any new App being registered in EntraID, hence I am skipping the detailed explanation.

In case you want to follow along the steps, then please refer to this blog  post. Here you can follow part 1 titled "Register an application with Microsoft Graph API"(all 16 bullet points, until "Setup Entra ID as a source system in IPS").

My final app in EntraID looked like this. I have also highlighted the properties which you will need in the next steps. So please take a note of them.

 

 And that it for EntraID.

 

SAP Identity Provisioning Service(SAP IPS)

Thats where the magic happen. Here we will bring both MS EntraID and SSC V2 together. 

Note: I am using an SAP IPS tenant embedded with SAP IAS. If your tenant is Standalone-Neo ones, then also the steps should remain the same.

Setup "Source" system

  • Navigate to "Identity Provisioning" --> Source Systems and Add a new source system.

 

 

 

  • Choose "Microsoft Entra ID ®" as system type

  • Under transformation, go to the "Code Editor" mode and Copy/Paste the default transformation from page https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-identity-services/cloud-identity-services/microsoft-entra-id?locale=en-US

Since my EntraID was passing the value as "Member" for the user type, which due to Transformation check on the target system(SSC V2) was causing user to be "skipped"(SSC V2 always needs userType as Employee by default). Hence I had to modify the transformation a bit to include "userType" as employee.

{
    "user": {
        "condition": "$.userPrincipalName EMPTY false",
        "mappings": [
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.id",
                "targetVariable": "entityIdSourceSystem"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.mailNickname",
                "optional": true,
                "targetPath": "$.externalId",
                "correlationAttribute": true
            },
            {
                "constant": "urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:core:2.0:User",
                "targetPath": "$.schemas[0]"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.mail",
                "targetPath": "$.emails[0].value",
                "correlationAttribute": true
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.userPrincipalName",
                "targetPath": "$.userName",
                "correlationAttribute": true
            },
            {
                "constant": "Employee",
                "targetPath": "$.userType"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.displayName",
                "optional": true,
                "targetPath": "$.displayName"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.givenName",
                "optional": true,
                "targetPath": "$.name.givenName"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.surname",
                "optional": true,
                "targetPath": "$.name.familyName"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.mobilePhone",
                "optional": true,
                "targetPath": "$.phoneNumbers[0].value",
                "defaultValue": "888-888-8888"
            },
            {
                "condition": "$.mobilePhone EMPTY false",
                "constant": "mobile",
                "targetPath": "$.phoneNumbers[0].type"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.businessPhones[0]",
                "optional": true,
                "targetPath": "$.phoneNumbers[1].value"
            },
            {
                "condition": "$.businessPhones.length() > 0",
                "constant": "work",
                "targetPath": "$.phoneNumbers[1].type"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.groups",
                "preserveArrayWithSingleElement": true,
                "optional": true,
                "targetPath": "$.groups"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.manager.id",
                "optional": true,
                "targetPath": "$['urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:enterprise:2.0:User']['manager']['value']"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.manager.displayName",
                "optional": true,
                "targetPath": "$['urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:enterprise:2.0:User']['manager']['displayName']"
            }
        ]
    },
    "group": {
        "ignore": false,
        "mappings": [
            {
                "constant": "urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:core:2.0:Group",
                "targetPath": "$.schemas[0]"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.id",
                "targetVariable": "entityIdSourceSystem"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.mailNickname",
                "optional": true,
                "targetPath": "$.externalId"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.displayName",
                "targetPath": "$.displayName"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.displayName",
                "targetPath": "$['urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:sap:2.0:Group']['externalName']"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.members",
                "preserveArrayWithSingleElement": true,
                "optional": true,
                "targetPath": "$.members"
            },
            {
                "constant": "value",
                "optional": true,
                "targetPath": "$.members[*].id",
                "type": "rename"
            },
            {
                "condition": "'%ips.application.id%' !== 'null'",
                "constant": "%ips.application.id%",
                "targetPath": "$['urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:sap:2.0:Group']['applicationId']"
            },
            {
                "constant": "userGroup",
                "targetPath": "$['urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:sap:2.0:Group']['type']"
            },
            {
                "constant": "readWrite",
                "targetPath": "$['urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:sap:2.0:Group']['supportedOperations']"
            }
        ]
    }
}
  • And next move to the properties tab and maintain the respective properties as shared in the official document. 

As I was facing few difficulties in the beginning, I have added a lot more properties then mentioned in the document. But this helped me to get the trace content in the provisioning log for the IPS. The one highlighted in yellow, can skipped and are there only for the trace purposes.

 Thats all for source system setup.

Setup "Target" System

  • Navigate to "Identity Provisioning" --> Target Systems and Add a new target system.

 

 

  • Choose "SAP Sales Cloud and SAP Service Cloud" as system type AND select the previously created "Source" from the drop down.

 

  • Under "Transformation" tab, switch to code editor and copy the same code as on standard help page under section "Default transformation for connector version 4".  

Again, I was still getting the error due to "primary" tag missing in the JSON body. Hence I modified the transformation a bit.

{
    "user": {
        "condition": "((('%c4c.group.prefix%' === 'null') || ($.groups[?(@.display =~ /%c4c.group.prefix%.*/)] empty false)) && $.userName empty false && ($.userType === 'Employee' || $.userType === 'employee'))",
        "mappings": [
            {
                "sourceVariable": "entityIdTargetSystem",
                "targetPath": "$.id"
            },
            {
                "constant": [
                    "urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:core:2.0:User",
                    "urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:sap:2.0:User",
                    "urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:enterprise:2.0:User"
                ],
                "targetPath": "$.schemas"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.userName",
                "targetPath": "$.userName"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.emails[0].value",
                "targetPath": "$.emails[0].value"
            },
            {
                "constant": "work",
                "targetPath": "$.emails[0].type"
            },
            {
                "constant": true,
                "targetPath": "$.emails[0].primary"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.active",
                "optional": true,
                "targetPath": "$.active"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.userType",
                "targetPath": "$.userType"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.displayName",
                "optional": true,
                "targetPath": "$.displayName"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.externalId",
                "optional": true,
                "targetPath": "$.externalId"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.name.givenName",
                "optional": true,
                "targetPath": "$.name.givenName"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.name.middleName",
                "optional": true,
                "targetPath": "$.name.middleName"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.name.familyName",
                "optional": true,
                "targetPath": "$.name.familyName"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.name.formatted",
                "optional": true,
                "targetPath": "$.name.formatted"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$['urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:sap:2.0:User']['userUuid']",
                "optional": true,
                "targetPath": "$['urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:sap:2.0:User']['userUuid']"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$['urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:sap:2.0:User']['validFrom']",
                "optional": true,
                "targetPath": "$['urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:sap:2.0:User']['validFrom']"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$['urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:sap:2.0:User']['validTo']",
                "optional": true,
                "targetPath": "$['urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:sap:2.0:User']['validTo']"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$['urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:sap:2.0:User']['status']",
                "optional": true,
                "targetPath": "$['urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:sap:2.0:User']['status']"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$['urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:enterprise:2.0:User']['employeeNumber']",
                "optional": true,
                "targetPath": "$['urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:enterprise:2.0:User']['employeeNumber']"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.locale",
                "optional": true,
                "targetPath": "$.locale"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.timezone",
                "optional": true,
                "targetPath": "$.timezone"
            }
        ]
    },
    "group": {
        "condition": "isAttributeWithOptionalPrefix($.displayName, c4c.group.prefix) && isAttributeWithOptionalPrefix($['urn:sap:cloud:scim:schemas:extension:custom:2.0:Group']['name'], c4c.group.prefix) && (isRegularGroup() || isApplicationSpecificGroup())",
        "mappings": [
            {
                "sourceVariable": "entityIdTargetSystem",
                "targetPath": "$.id"
            },
            {
                "constant": [
                    "urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:core:2.0:Group",
                    "urn:ietf:params:scim:schemas:extension:sap:2.0:Group"
                ],
                "targetPath": "$.schemas"
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.displayName",
                "targetPath": "$.displayName",
                "functions": [
                    {
                        "function": "replaceFirstString",
                        "condition": "isAttributeWithMandatoryPrefix(@, c4c.group.prefix)",
                        "regex": "%c4c.group.prefix%",
                        "replacement": ""
                    }
                ]
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$['urn:sap:cloud:scim:schemas:extension:custom:2.0:Group']['name']",
                "optional": true,
                "targetPath": "$.displayName",
                "functions": [
                    {
                        "function": "replaceFirstString",
                        "condition": "isAttributeWithMandatoryPrefix(@, c4c.group.prefix)",
                        "regex": "%c4c.group.prefix%",
                        "replacement": ""
                    }
                ]
            },
            {
                "sourcePath": "$.members[*].value",
                "preserveArrayWithSingleElement": true,
                "optional": true,
                "targetPath": "$.members[?(@.value)]",
                "functions": [
                    {
                        "function": "resolveEntityIds"
                    }
                ]
            }
        ]
    }
}
  • And next move to the properties tab and maintain the respective properties as shared in the official document. Here while I have skiped the trace related property, you are free to add them if needed.

 And that's it.  

 

Testing

In order to test, you will need to execute IPS Provisioning jobs.

  • Navigate to your "Source" system and go to "Jobs" Tab.

If you are testing for the first time, my recommendation would be, try first the simulate and then observe the results in Job Logs. If needed, you can further write your filtering login in the "Source Transformation" go get only relevant users in SSC V2.

PS: Dont forget to subscribe to get the background job status in your(or colleagues) emails inbox.

 When you simulate then you can go to job logs and see the results:

  • If simulation results are fine, then trigger "Read Job" and then, just like "Simulate Job", you can go to job logs and see the results
  • And once you are satisfied with the results, schedule the jobs periodically based on the business need. 

Watchout: Once an user is created in SSC V2, it cannt be deleted. Hence be careful with the actual read job !!! 

https://me.sap.com/notes/3474746 

 

Logs & Troubleshooting

SAP IPS provides an inbuilt job logs for monitoring & troubleshooting. You can navigate to "Provisioning Logs" and look for the respective instance of the job to dig into its log.

Of course, if you enabled the "trace" related properties in the respective source/target system then, you can also see the content/payload of the instance failing.

Note that these jobs logs are very short lived. Hence, if needed, store them locally to analyse.

 

Move change to QA/Production

Unfortunately, There is not automated way to move your changed to production. You will need to download the respective Source/Target system(along with respective transformations additionally) and then import in the QA/Production stage.

And then import from file in the target stage.

 

Summary

As can be seen, it is a very lengthy setup, but at the same time it is very well documented in bits & pieces. Hence if you follow it throughly E2E then I am confident that you will have a running or working setup in no more than a few days. 

And dont forget... donot run the "read job" without simulations and validation of the results, or else be ready to get your SSC V2 dumped with 100s or 1000s of irrelevant users!

Let me know your feedback/comments/questions. 

Best Regards
Saurabh
https://www.linkedin.com/in/saurabhkabra2009/

entry.title: Re: unwanted redirection to community home page
entry.content: I wonder if there's a motive behind this behavior. I mean, that's not how pages usually work. So, was this done intentionally? To what end?

entry.title: Humanoidd robots are a catalyst, not the solution
entry.content:
Before talking about humanoids, let's look at some facts about humans.

If you're not a program reading this, you can do so with eyes which see much better than the eyes of many animals — but are no match for the detail an eagle can make out.

If you listen to this text, your ears allow you to do so much better than the ears of a tortoise, but you're no match to your dog. And as we're talking about dogs: if they have a certain size, don't try to outrun them. You are not too bad at running, but still — many predators are faster than you.

The one thing humans do better than most animals is long-duration slow running — your dog might be the only animal which joins you on a marathon run (but please protect the paws).

Energy consumption is another topic which often gets overlooked. A human body operates on 2–3.5 kWh per day — a humanoid robot doing bodily work consumes that energy in one hour. And while the batteries need electrons to recharge, you can eat some fruit, vegetables or meat to recharge yours.

The human is, overall, a good and versatile compromise, fitting well into an existing ecosystem of resources. Our weird way of walking on two legs — which combines a high center of gravity with two fragile and unstable connections to the ground, requires permanent adjustment of balance and a complex concept of sensing movement and orientation, combined with immediate muscular reaction — enables us to do a number of different things in a mediocre way. We're the experts in doing many things "ok" and few things as the best.

So why do we want to re-engineer this type of machine and create humanoid robots?

I think the old dream of humans to create an artificial "human" which does the work we don't want to do is probably at the core of the movement. Being able to replicate human motions, human activity and having a machine doing human tasks is something we (as humanity) have been dreaming about since "ever" (have a look at Gods and Robots).

Probably the idea of having companions who simply do what you ask them to do is another attractive perspective — a humanoid won't complain if you ask them to bring out the trash or to clean up the kitchen (though we are still a few steps away from that level of accuracy).

Where's the evolution right now?

Creating mechanical solutions to make machines able to move like humans is a considerable effort. Our mechanics are the results of our own evolution, and trying to recreate how joints, limbs and ligaments work is possible — because we have a template — but takes time nonetheless. If you look at how robots are moving today, you come to realize that the human body is a quite impressive machine.

When we then discuss the use of humanoids in business, another question comes up: why make a compromise with a human-like, complex-to-navigate machine instead of creating machines which are built for one purpose and do that very well?

To my surprise, the answer comes from a humanoid robot manufacturer itself: in R&D, they manually assemble lot sizes of up to 10 in an unstructured, engineering-focused approach. Lot sizes from 10 to 100 are assembled by humans (!) in a manufacturing site designed for — humans. Lot sizes of 100 and above are outsourced into an automated production facility, where dedicated machinery (not humanoids) will assemble the humanoids.

Replace humans with humanoids in that scenario and you have the answer to where humanoids are the right fit: when you need flexibility and do not need to scale. If you've ever looked at industrial production, you know that there are many places in which production processes are not integrated and a "human interface" is transporting components from process step to process step — either because the process is not executed often enough to justify a real integration (like a conveyor belt or a robot arm), or because the step requires complex handling or movements and would be too expensive to automate.

So what we are doing is teaching machines to stand upright, to perform human tasks, to talk. And we're getting better and better, extremely fast. Today's humanoid robots move more smoothly with every software and hardware upgrade, get more run time out of their batteries and can use AI to react to unforeseen situations.

They will — and have to — become faster, but that's only a matter of time. Still, a purpose-built machine using modern technologies will always be faster when it comes to scaling. That said, humanoid robot development is key to advancing the technology of robotics and the combination of robotics and AI.

And in cases where you want to start automating things you could not automate before — because of complexity or low volume — humanoid robots can be the path to get started.

The other big advantage of humanoids is that they democratize automation. Right now, "localizing" robots requires specialist involvement. In a few years, this learning of layouts and processes will be powered by AI and make robotics an accessible technology for a much larger set of companies.

Humanoid robots are a segway into a new era of automation and will stay the tool of choice in the niche of innovation, flexibility and low-volume production.

If you want to learn more about this technology and develop your own robotics use case, join our Embodied AI Jam in Switzerland in August.

entry.title: Re: SAP Digital Mfg: "Create HU" button missing in GR dia...
entry.content: Make sure EWM setup is in place, including Storage Location configured as EWM-managed in DM.
